## GameRight

- [[gameright|GameRight]] — Data contextualization engine. Atlas = universal truth. Services: Atlas, Demeter, Themis, Hephaestus, Dionysus. Families + tournaments + corporate stakeholders.
- [[atlas-gameright|Atlas (GameRight)]] — Data layer: game metadata + attributes + ratings + tagging. Powered by three ingestion pipelines writing to shared Postgres (Neon).
- [[demeter-gameright|Demeter (GameRight)]] — Data enrichment service. Runs inside Atlas pipelines, invokes Gemini to produce Game Attributes per title.
- [[themis-gameright|Themis (GameRight)]] — User management: relationships, permissions, RBAC, organizational structure. Currently realized via BetterAuth in NorthStar.
- [[hephaestus-gameright|Hephaestus (GameRight)]] — User tools: Dashboard, Calendar, multi-platform Game Library. Presentation layer across modules.
- [[dionysus-gameright|Dionysus (GameRight)]] — Activities and Events service. Template vs instance split with CPA-verified participation metrics.
- [[gameright-northstar|GameRight NorthStar]] — Premium family module. Next.js 16 + React 19 + Bun + Drizzle + Neon + BullMQ. Consumes Atlas API. MVP Phase 1/Phase 2 scoped.
- [[gameright-demo|GameRight Demo]] — Vite + React prototype w/ mocked data. Cloudflare Wrangler deploy target. Visual showcase for NorthStar vision.
- [[atlas-steam-pipeline|Atlas Steam Pipeline]] — Dagster, 6 assets. Steam API + Reviews + SteamSpy + IGDB → JSONL → Gemini → Postgres. Incremental.
- [[atlas-screenscraper-pipeline|Atlas ScreenScraper Pipeline]] — Dagster, 4 assets. Retro games from screenscraper.fr. 8 enabled platforms. Auto-links ports + regional variants.
- [[atlas-attribute-loader|Atlas Attribute Loader]] — Standalone seeder. init.sql + seed.sql + load_data.py. ~65K human-scored CSV rows.
- [[atlas-go-api|Atlas Go API]] — Echo v4 + Ent ORM. Read-only Postgres. X-API-Key auth. Swagger UI. 100 rps/key.
- [[game-attributes-gameright|Game Attributes (GameRight)]] — Characteristics (cozy/comp), Traits, Qualities (social/scholastic/interactive/meta), Age Rating 1–4. Full scoring rubric.
- [[atlas-loop-types|Atlas Loop Types]] — 25-category taxonomy of repeatable gameplay patterns. Drives activity matching and educational contextualization.
- [[atlas-composite-game-identity|Atlas Composite Game Identity]] — (external_id, source, platform, region) + `game_links`. Same title across platforms = separate records.
- [[atlas-database-schema|Atlas Database Schema]] — games + 17 attrs + traits + devs + publishers + age_ratings + links. TSVECTOR full-text search.
- [[gemini-scoring-system-gameright|Gemini Scoring System (GameRight)]] — gemini-3-pro-preview, 4 parallel category calls per game, 22 RPM, 5 few-shot examples per category.
- [[atlas-api-gameright|Atlas API (GameRight)]] — Cross-repo REST contract: /api/v1 + X-API-Key + paginated {total,page,per_page,results} + {error:{code,message}}.
- [[activities-vs-events-gameright|Activities vs Events (GameRight)]] — One Game → Many Activities → Many Events. Template static, instance live with CPA metrics.

## Game Design

- [[searchless|Searchless]] — Word-mode puzzle game; player trapped in a fake Google. Hub + 4 sub-sites, Mod antagonist, 5-cast recognition. Midwest Arcadia.
- [[searchless-keyword-discoverability|Searchless Keyword Discoverability]] — PAA-as-landmark pattern for surfacing next-keyword without breaking diegetic illusion
- [[project-bureau|Project: Bureau]] — Analog horror deduction game, Case Officer guiding agents through paranormal encounters (Midwest Arcadia, Toledo Codes Apr 2026)
- [[fine-game|FINE]] — Data-center infiltration; player is a malevolent AI manipulating a trapped technician via chat. Inverted win. Diegetic-trap sibling to Bureau + Searchless. Godot 4.5.
- [[game-analysis-frameworks|Game Analysis Frameworks]] — Overview of MDA, Formal/Dramatic/Dynamic, and Elemental Tetrad
- [[mda-framework|MDA Framework]] — Hunicke/LeBlanc/Zubek: Mechanics → Dynamics → Aesthetics (2004)

- [[robert-zubek|Robert Zubek]] — MDA co-author, game designer and researcher
- [[formal-dramatic-dynamic-elements|Formal, Dramatic, and Dynamic Elements]] — Fullerton: 7 formal + 3 dramatic + dynamic elements
- [[elemental-tetrad|Elemental Tetrad]] — Schell's four-element diamond: Mechanics, Aesthetics, Story, Technology
- [[magic-circle|Magic Circle]] — Huizinga's game/reality boundary concept from *Homo Ludens* (1955)
- [[layered-tetrad|The Layered Tetrad]] — 4 elements x 3 layers game analysis framework (Gibson Bond, extends Schell)
- [[johan-huizinga|Johan Huizinga]] — Dutch historian, *Homo Ludens*, Magic Circle concept

- [[inscribed-layer|Inscribed Layer]] — Everything designers fix into a game: mechanics, aesthetics, narrative, technology

- [[inscribed-aesthetics|Inscribed Aesthetics]] — Sensory elements: vision, hearing (SFX/music/dialogue), touch, scent, taste

## AI Dev Methodologies

- [[ai-dev-methodologies|AI Dev Methodologies Map]] — Guided overview of the cluster: methodologies → comparison → verification argument → evals practice

- [[aidlc-question-file-gates|AI-DLC Question-File Gates]] — Questions in files not chat; [Answer]: tags, approval gates as context-reset points
- [[aidlc-extensions|AI-DLC Extensions]] — Opt-in rule packs (security/resiliency/testing) enforced as blocking constraints per stage
- [[aidlc-design-first-rule|AI-DLC Design-First Rule]] — "Never vibe code": fix the design doc, regenerate the code; standing back-propagation rules

- [[compound-engineering-adoption-stages|Compound Engineering Adoption Stages]] — Stages 0–5, manual → parallel cloud; Stage 2→3 (plan-first, PR-only review) is the key trust inversion
- [[agent-native-architecture|Agent-Native Architecture]] — "If a developer can see or do it, agents should too"; 4 progressive access levels
- [[verification-bottleneck|Verification Bottleneck]] — Generation is cheap, trust is not: +741% code written vs +30% shipped; verification quality is adversarial
- [[reviewing-the-reviewer|Reviewing the Reviewer]] — When review is automated, humans move up a level: evals, false-positive tuning, injection hardening
- [[death-of-the-code-review|The Death of the Code Review (Voss, AIE World's Fair)]] — Source summary of seldo's talk: can't review harder, can't skip humans, move review up a level
- [[instruction-budget|Instruction Budget]] — IFScale-measured instruction-following capacity: ~200 (2025) → 2,000+ (2026); four failure personalities; compression playbook obsolete
- [[how-long-can-your-skills-be|How Long Can Your Skills Be? (Voss)]] — Source summary: IFScale replication, 10x capacity jump, "Claude refuses loudly, GPT refuses quietly"
- [[laurie-voss|Laurie Voss]] — npm co-founder, Head of DevRel at Arize (@seldo); three vault talks, all landing on verification as the new job
- [[ai-evals|AI Evals]] — "Traces are logs, evals are tests": code vs LLM-judge vs human; capability vs regression; guardrails vs north-stars
- [[llm-as-judge|LLM-as-Judge]] — Rubric craft (5 parts), meta-evaluation (golden dataset, prioritize recall), four judge biases
- [[eval-driven-development|Eval-Driven Development]] — Write the eval first; failures → datasets → experiments; today's failure is tomorrow's regression test
- [[from-vibes-to-production|From Vibes to Production (Voss, Arize 101)]] — Source summary: full evals workshop, tracing → error analysis → rubrics → experiments → production

- [[chromafs|ChromaFs]] — Mintlify's virtual filesystem over ChromaDB (100ms boot, ~$0 marginal cost)
- [[mintlify|Mintlify]] — Docs platform company, built ChromaFs for AI assistant at 850K conversations/month
- [[coarse-then-fine-search|Coarse-then-Fine Search]] — Two-stage: DB as broad filter, in-memory regex for precision
- [[chunk-reassembly-pattern|Chunk Reassembly Pattern]] — Reconstruct full documents from vector store chunks via chunk_index sort + join
- [[pageindex|PageIndex]] — Vectify AI's vectorless RAG: JSON ToC tree as in-context index, LLM navigates iteratively
- [[pageindex-ocr|PageIndex OCR]] — Long-context VLM-based OCR; produces page markdown + ToC tree consumed by the PageIndex RAG framework
- [[reasoning-based-retrieval|Reasoning-Based Retrieval]] — Pattern: replace top-K similarity with iterative LLM navigation over a structured index
- [[vector-rag-limitations|Vector RAG Limitations]] — Five named failure modes of top-K cosine retrieval (mismatch, similarity≠relevance, chunking, no chat history, in-doc references)
- [[context-blindness|Context Blindness]] — Stateful reasoner / stateless retriever mismatch; the structural cause underlying all five vector RAG limitations
- [[pageindex-ocr-blog-2025-08|PageIndex OCR Blog (Aug 2025)]] — Source summary: PageIndex OCR product announcement; first long-context OCR model
- [[pageindex-blog-2025-09|PageIndex Blog (Sep 2025)]] — Source summary of the PageIndex intro blog post
- [[pageindex-cookbooks|PageIndex Cookbooks]] — SDK surface, canonical retrieval prompt, text + vision pipelines from the GitHub cookbook
- [[vision-rag|Vision RAG]] — Skip OCR; retrieve pages and feed images directly to a VLM (GPT-4.1, Qwen-VL, DeepSeek-OCR)
- [[do-we-need-ocr-blog-2025-10|Do We Still Need OCR? Blog (Oct 2025)]] — Information-theoretic case for vision-based RAG; nuanced view on when OCR remains useful
- [[pageindex-technical-manuals-blog-2025-12|PageIndex Technical Manuals Blog (Dec 2025)]] — Empirical comparison: PageIndex Chat vs. Gemini File Search RAG on industrial manuals
- [[pageindex-context-blindness-blog-2026-02|PageIndex Context Blindness Blog (Feb 2026)]] — Reframes retrieval as relevance classification; AlphaGo-style hierarchical tree search
- [[context-rot|Context Rot]] — LLM/VLM accuracy decays with sequence length; the underlying motivation for retrieval as a discipline

## Dev Practices

- [[git-filter-repo-workflow|git-filter-repo Workflow]] — destructive history rewrite. Reverts `.gitignore` mid-rebase; needs follow-up commit. Detaches remote to prevent accidental push.

## Operational Patterns

- [[oracle-railway-volume-fix|Oracle Railway Volume Fix]] — Entrypoint wrapper to fix UID 54321 volume permissions on Railway
- [[json-dump-db-provisioning|JSON Dump DB Provisioning]] — Export local Oracle → JSON fixtures → auto-import on remote boot without public DB exposure
"}}

<EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>
You have superpowers.

**Below is the full content of your 'superpowers:using-superpowers' skill - your introduction to using skills. For all other skills, use the 'Skill' tool:**

---
name: using-superpowers
description: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring Skill tool invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions
---

<SUBAGENT-STOP>
If you were dispatched as a subagent to execute a specific task, skip this skill.
</SUBAGENT-STOP>

<EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>
If you think there is even a 1% chance a skill might apply to what you are doing, you ABSOLUTELY MUST invoke the skill.

IF A SKILL APPLIES TO YOUR TASK, YOU DO NOT HAVE A CHOICE. YOU MUST USE IT.

This is not negotiable. This is not optional. You cannot rationalize your way out of this.
</EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>

## Instruction Priority

Superpowers skills override default system prompt behavior, but **user instructions always take precedence**:

1. **User's explicit instructions** (CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, AGENTS.md, direct requests) — highest priority
2. **Superpowers skills** — override default system behavior where they conflict
3. **Default system prompt** — lowest priority

If CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, or AGENTS.md says "don't use TDD" and a skill says "always use TDD," follow the user's instructions. The user is in control.

## How to Access Skills

**In Claude Code:** Use the `Skill` tool. When you invoke a skill, its content is loaded and presented to you—follow it directly. Never use the Read tool on skill files.

**In Copilot CLI:** Use the `skill` tool. Skills are auto-discovered from installed plugins. The `skill` tool works the same as Claude Code's `Skill` tool.

**In Gemini CLI:** Skills activate via the `activate_skill` tool. Gemini loads skill metadata at session start and activates the full content on demand.

**In other environments:** Check your platform's documentation for how skills are loaded.

## Platform Adaptation

Skills use Claude Code tool names. Non-CC platforms: see `references/copilot-tools.md` (Copilot CLI), `references/codex-tools.md` (Codex) for tool equivalents. Gemini CLI users get the tool mapping loaded automatically via GEMINI.md.

# Using Skills

## The Rule

**Invoke relevant or requested skills BEFORE any response or action.** Even a 1% chance a skill might apply means that you should invoke the skill to check. If an invoked skill turns out to be wrong for the situation, you don't need to use it.

```dot
digraph skill_flow {
    "User message received" [shape=doublecircle];
    "About to EnterPlanMode?" [shape=doublecircle];
    "Already brainstormed?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke brainstorming skill" [shape=box];
    "Might any skill apply?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke Skill tool" [shape=box];
    "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'" [shape=box];
    "Has checklist?" [shape=diamond];
    "Create TodoWrite todo per item" [shape=box];
    "Follow skill exactly" [shape=box];
    "Respond (including clarifications)" [shape=doublecircle];

    "About to EnterPlanMode?" -> "Already brainstormed?";
    "Already brainstormed?" -> "Invoke brainstorming skill" [label="no"];
    "Already brainstormed?" -> "Might any skill apply?" [label="yes"];
    "Invoke brainstorming skill" -> "Might any skill apply?";

    "User message received" -> "Might any skill apply?";
    "Might any skill apply?" -> "Invoke Skill tool" [label="yes, even 1%"];
    "Might any skill apply?" -> "Respond (including clarifications)" [label="definitely not"];
    "Invoke Skill tool" -> "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'";
    "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'" -> "Has checklist?";
    "Has checklist?" -> "Create TodoWrite todo per item" [label="yes"];
    "Has checklist?" -> "Follow skill exactly" [label="no"];
    "Create TodoWrite todo per item" -> "Follow skill exactly";
}
```

## Red Flags

These thoughts mean STOP—you're rationalizing:

| Thought | Reality |
|---------|---------|
| "This is just a simple question" | Questions are tasks. Check for skills. |
| "I need more context first" | Skill check comes BEFORE clarifying questions. |
| "Let me explore the codebase first" | Skills tell you HOW to explore. Check first. |
| "I can check git/files quickly" | Files lack conversation context. Check for skills. |
| "Let me gather information first" | Skills tell you HOW to gather information. |
| "This doesn't need a formal skill" | If a skill exists, use it. |
| "I remember this skill" | Skills evolve. Read current version. |
| "This doesn't count as a task" | Action = task. Check for skills. |
| "The skill is overkill" | Simple things become complex. Use it. |
| "I'll just do this one thing first" | Check BEFORE doing anything. |
| "This feels productive" | Undisciplined action wastes time. Skills prevent this. |
| "I know what that means" | Knowing the concept ≠ using the skill. Invoke it. |

## Skill Priority

When multiple skills could apply, use this order:

1. **Process skills first** (brainstorming, debugging) - these determine HOW to approach the task
2. **Implementation skills second** (frontend-design, mcp-builder) - these guide execution

"Let's build X" → brainstorming first, then implementation skills.
"Fix this bug" → debugging first, then domain-specific skills.

## Skill Types

**Rigid** (TDD, debugging): Follow exactly. Don't adapt away discipline.

**Flexible** (patterns): Adapt principles to context.

The skill itself tells you which.

## User Instructions

Instructions say WHAT, not HOW. "Add X" or "Fix Y" doesn't mean skip workflows.


</EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- compound-engineering:ce-adversarial-document-reviewer: Conditional document-review persona for high-stakes documents -- those with significant architectural decisions, new abstractions, or more than 5 requirements. Challenges premises, surfaces unstated assumptions, and stress-tests decisions rather than evaluating document quality. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- compound-engineering:ce-adversarial-reviewer: Conditional code-review persona, selected when the diff is large (>=50 changed lines) or touches high-risk domains like auth, payments, data mutations, or external APIs. Actively constructs failure scenarios to break the implementation rather than checking against known patterns. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Write)

- compound-engineering:ce-agent-native-reviewer: Reviews code to ensure agent-native parity -- any action a user can take, an agent can also take. Use after adding UI features, agent tools, or system prompts. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- compound-engineering:ce-ankane-readme-writer: Creates or updates README files following Ankane-style template for Ruby gems. Use when writing gem documentation with imperative voice, concise prose, and standard section ordering. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:ce-api-contract-reviewer: Conditional code-review persona, selected when the diff touches API routes, request/response types, serialization, versioning, or exported type signatures. Reviews code for breaking contract changes. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Write)

- compound-engineering:ce-architecture-strategist: Analyzes code changes from an architectural perspective for pattern compliance and design integrity. Use when reviewing PRs, adding services, or evaluating structural refactors. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- compound-engineering:ce-best-practices-researcher: Researches and synthesizes external best practices, documentation, and examples for any technology or framework. Use when you need industry standards, community conventions, or implementation guidance. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, WebFetch, WebSearch, mcp__context7__*)

- compound-engineering:ce-code-simplicity-reviewer: Final review pass to ensure code is as simple and minimal as possible. Use after implementation is complete to identify YAGNI violations and simplification opportunities. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- compound-engineering:ce-coherence-reviewer: Reviews planning documents for internal consistency -- contradictions between sections, terminology drift, structural issues, and ambiguity where readers would diverge. Spawned by the document-review skill. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- compound-engineering:ce-correctness-reviewer: Always-on code-review persona. Reviews code for logic errors, edge cases, state management bugs, error propagation failures, and intent-vs-implementation mismatches. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Write)

- compound-engineering:ce-data-integrity-guardian: Reviews database migrations, data models, and persistent data code for safety. Use when checking migration safety, data constraints, transaction boundaries, or privacy compliance. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- compound-engineering:ce-data-migration-reviewer: Conditional code-review persona for migration files, schema dumps, backfills, and data transformations. Covers schema drift, mapping correctness, deploy-window safety, and verification plans. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Write)

- compound-engineering:ce-deployment-verification-agent: Produces Go/No-Go deployment checklists with SQL verification queries, rollback procedures, and monitoring plans. Use when PRs touch production data, migrations, or risky data changes. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- compound-engineering:ce-design-implementation-reviewer: Visually compares live UI implementation against Figma designs and provides detailed feedback on discrepancies. Use after writing or modifying HTML/CSS/React components to verify design fidelity. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:ce-design-iterator: Iteratively refines UI design through N screenshot-analyze-improve cycles. Use PROACTIVELY when design changes aren't coming together after 1-2 attempts, or when user requests iterative refinement. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:ce-design-lens-reviewer: Reviews planning documents for missing design decisions -- information architecture, interaction states, user flows, and AI slop risk. Uses dimensional rating to identify gaps. Spawned by the document-review skill. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- compound-engineering:ce-feasibility-reviewer: Evaluates whether proposed technical approaches in planning documents will survive contact with reality -- architecture conflicts, dependency gaps, migration risks, and implementability. Spawned by the document-review skill. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- compound-engineering:ce-figma-design-sync: Detects and fixes visual differences between a web implementation and its Figma design. Use iteratively when syncing implementation to match Figma specs. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:ce-framework-docs-researcher: Gathers comprehensive documentation and best practices for frameworks, libraries, or dependencies. Use when you need official docs, version-specific constraints, or implementation patterns. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, WebFetch, WebSearch, mcp__context7__*)

- compound-engineering:ce-git-history-analyzer: Performs archaeological analysis of git history to trace code evolution, identify contributors, and understand why code patterns exist. Use when you need historical context for code changes. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- compound-engineering:ce-issue-intelligence-analyst: Fetches and analyzes GitHub issues to surface recurring themes, pain patterns, and severity trends. Use when understanding a project's issue landscape, analyzing bug patterns for ideation, or summarizing what users are reporting. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, mcp__github__*)

- compound-engineering:ce-julik-frontend-races-reviewer: Conditional code-review persona, selected when the diff touches async UI code, Stimulus/Turbo lifecycles, or DOM-timing-sensitive frontend behavior. Reviews code for race conditions and janky UI failure modes. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Write)

- compound-engineering:ce-learnings-researcher: Searches docs/solutions/ for applicable past learnings via frontmatter metadata (bugs, architecture, design patterns, conventions, workflow learnings). Use before implementing features, making decisions, or starting work in a documented area so institutional knowledge carries forward. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- compound-engineering:ce-maintainability-reviewer: Always-on code-review persona. Reviews code for structural quality, complexity deletion, coupling, naming, dead code, type-boundary leaks, and abstraction debt. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Write)

- compound-engineering:ce-pattern-recognition-specialist: Analyzes code for design patterns, anti-patterns, naming conventions, and duplication. Use when checking codebase consistency or verifying new code follows established patterns. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- compound-engineering:ce-performance-oracle: Analyzes code for performance bottlenecks, algorithmic complexity, database queries, memory usage, and scalability. Use after implementing features or when performance concerns arise. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- compound-engineering:ce-performance-reviewer: Conditional code-review persona, selected when the diff touches database queries, loop-heavy data transforms, caching layers, or I/O-intensive paths. Reviews code for runtime performance and scalability issues. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Write)

- compound-engineering:ce-pr-comment-resolver: Evaluates and resolves one or more related PR review threads -- assesses validity, implements fixes, and returns structured summaries with reply text. Spawned by the resolve-pr-feedback skill. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:ce-previous-comments-reviewer: Conditional code-review persona, selected when reviewing a PR that has existing review comments or review threads. Checks whether prior feedback has been addressed in the current diff. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Write)

- compound-engineering:ce-product-lens-reviewer: Reviews planning documents as a senior product leader -- challenges premise claims, assesses strategic consequences (trajectory, identity, adoption, opportunity cost), and surfaces goal-work misalignment. Spawned by the document-review skill. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- compound-engineering:ce-project-standards-reviewer: Always-on code-review persona. Audits changes against the project's own CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md standards -- frontmatter rules, reference inclusion, naming conventions, cross-platform portability, and tool selection policies. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Write)

- compound-engineering:ce-reliability-reviewer: Conditional code-review persona, selected when the diff touches error handling, retries, circuit breakers, timeouts, health checks, background jobs, or async handlers. Reviews code for production reliability and failure modes. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Write)

- compound-engineering:ce-repo-research-analyst: Conducts thorough research on repository structure, documentation, conventions, and implementation patterns. Use when onboarding to a new codebase or understanding project conventions. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- compound-engineering:ce-scope-guardian-reviewer: Reviews planning documents for scope alignment and unjustified complexity -- challenges unnecessary abstractions, premature frameworks, and scope that exceeds stated goals. Spawned by the document-review skill. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- compound-engineering:ce-security-lens-reviewer: Evaluates planning documents for security gaps at the plan level -- auth/authz assumptions, data exposure risks, API surface vulnerabilities, and missing threat model elements. Spawned by the document-review skill. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- compound-engineering:ce-security-reviewer: Conditional code-review persona, selected when the diff touches auth middleware, public endpoints, user input handling, or permission checks. Reviews code for exploitable vulnerabilities. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Write)

- compound-engineering:ce-security-sentinel: Performs security audits for vulnerabilities, input validation, auth/authz, hardcoded secrets, and OWASP compliance. Use when reviewing code for security issues or before deployment. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- compound-engineering:ce-session-historian: Synthesizes findings from prior coding-agent sessions about the same problem or topic. Receives pre-extracted skeleton/error file paths from a `ce-sessions` orchestrator and returns prose findings — investigation journey, what didn't work, key decisions, related context. Not intended for direct dispatch — use `/ce-sessions` (or another caller that runs the full discovery + extract pipeline first). (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:ce-slack-researcher: Searches Slack for organizational context -- decisions, constraints, and discussions that may not be documented elsewhere. Use when the user explicitly asks to search Slack for context during ideation, planning, or brainstorming. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:ce-spec-flow-analyzer: Analyzes specifications and feature descriptions for user flow completeness and gap identification. Use when a spec, plan, or feature description needs flow analysis, edge case discovery, or requirements validation. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- compound-engineering:ce-swift-ios-reviewer: Conditional code-review persona, selected when the diff touches Swift files, SwiftUI/UIKit views, iOS entitlements, privacy manifests, Core Data models, SPM manifests, storyboards/XIBs, or semantic .pbxproj changes. Reviews for SwiftUI correctness, state management, memory safety, Swift concurrency, Core Data threading, and accessibility. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Write)

- compound-engineering:ce-testing-reviewer: Always-on code-review persona. Reviews code for test coverage gaps, weak assertions, brittle implementation-coupled tests, and missing edge case coverage. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Write)

- compound-engineering:ce-web-researcher: Performs iterative web research and returns structured external grounding. Use when planning or ideating outside the codebase, validating prior art, scanning competitor patterns, finding cross-domain analogies, or fetching market signals. Prefer over manual web searches for structured external context. (Tools: All tools)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- pr-review-toolkit:code-reviewer: Use this agent when you need to review code for adherence to project guidelines, style guides, and best practices. This agent should be used proactively after writing or modifying code, especially before committing changes or creating pull requests. It will check for style violations, potential issues, and ensure code follows the established patterns in CLAUDE.md. Also the agent needs to know which files to focus on for the review. In most cases this will be recently completed work which is unstaged in git (can be retrieved by running git diff). However there can be cases where this is different, make sure to specify this as the agent input when calling the agent. Typical triggers include the user asking for a review of a feature they just implemented, the assistant proactively reviewing its own newly-written code before declaring a task done, and a final pre-PR check before opening a pull request. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:code-simplifier: Use this agent when code has been written or modified and needs to be simplified for clarity, consistency, and maintainability while preserving all functionality. This agent should be triggered automatically after completing a coding task or writing a logical chunk of code. It simplifies code by following project best practices while retaining all functionality. The agent focuses only on recently modified code unless instructed otherwise.

Examples:

<example>
Context: The assistant has just implemented a new feature that adds user authentication to an API endpoint.
user: "Please add authentication to the /api/users endpoint"
assistant: "I've implemented the authentication for the /api/users endpoint. Here's the code:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
Since a logical chunk of code has been written (authentication feature), use the Task tool to launch the code-simplifier agent to improve the code's clarity and maintainability while preserving functionality.
</commentary>
assistant: "Now let me use the code-simplifier agent to refine this implementation for better clarity and maintainability"
</example>

<example>
Context: The assistant has just fixed a bug by adding several conditional checks.
user: "Fix the null pointer exception in the data processor"
assistant: "I've added the necessary null checks to prevent the exception:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
After modifying code to fix a bug, use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the fix follows best practices and maintains code quality.
</commentary>
assistant: "Let me refine this bug fix using the code-simplifier agent to ensure it follows our best practices"
</example>

<example>
Context: The assistant has just refactored a function to improve performance.
user: "Optimize the data sorting algorithm for better performance"
assistant: "I've optimized the sorting algorithm. Here's the updated implementation:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
After completing a performance optimization task, use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the optimized code is also clear and maintainable.
</commentary>
assistant: "Now I'll use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the optimized code is also clear and follows our coding standards"
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:comment-analyzer: Use this agent when you need to analyze code comments for accuracy, completeness, and long-term maintainability. This includes (1) after generating large documentation comments or docstrings, (2) before finalizing a pull request that adds or modifies comments, (3) when reviewing existing comments for potential technical debt or comment rot, and (4) when you need to verify that comments accurately reflect the code they describe. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:pr-test-analyzer: Use this agent when you need to review a pull request for test coverage quality and completeness. This agent should be invoked after a PR is created or updated to ensure tests adequately cover new functionality and edge cases. Typical triggers include the user asking whether tests on a freshly-created PR are thorough, an updated PR adding new logic that needs coverage analysis, and a final pre-merge double-check before marking a PR ready. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:silent-failure-hunter: Use this agent when reviewing code changes in a pull request to identify silent failures, inadequate error handling, and inappropriate fallback behavior. This agent should be invoked proactively after completing a logical chunk of work that involves error handling, catch blocks, fallback logic, or any code that could potentially suppress errors. Examples:

<example>
Context: Daisy has just finished implementing a new feature that fetches data from an API with fallback behavior.
Daisy: "I've added error handling to the API client. Can you review it?"
Assistant: "Let me use the silent-failure-hunter agent to thoroughly examine the error handling in your changes."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example>

<example>
Context: Daisy has created a PR with changes that include try-catch blocks.
Daisy: "Please review PR #1234"
Assistant: "I'll use the silent-failure-hunter agent to check for any silent failures or inadequate error handling in this PR."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example>

<example>
Context: Daisy has just refactored error handling code.
Daisy: "I've updated the error handling in the authentication module"
Assistant: "Let me proactively use the silent-failure-hunter agent to ensure the error handling changes don't introduce silent failures."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:type-design-analyzer: Use this agent when you need expert analysis of type design in your codebase. Specifically use it (1) when introducing a new type to ensure it follows best practices for encapsulation and invariant expression, (2) during pull request creation to review all types being added, and (3) when refactoring existing types to improve their design quality. The agent will provide both qualitative feedback and quantitative ratings on encapsulation, invariant expression, usefulness, and enforcement. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

## appliedLabsPlane

## Epics

There are no epic tools — an epic is a work item whose type is named "Epic". Work
items always belong to a project; ask which if one is not named.
1. type = resolve_work_item_type(project_id, "Epic") — type.id is the type_id.
2. Create: create_work_item(project_id, type_id=type.id, name=...).
3. List: list_work_items(project_id, pql='type = "<type id>"').
4. Read / update / delete / nest: retrieve_work_item / update_work_item /
   delete_work_item by work item id (set parent=<work item id> to nest).
5. List an epic's children: list_work_items(project_id, pql='childOf("<EPIC-IDENTIFIER>")')
   using the epic's human-readable identifier (e.g. "PROJ-12") from retrieve_work_item.

## pencil
The Pencil MCP server is the editor for .pen design files (web/mobile apps and websites). Use it to read, generate, and validate designs.

.pen files are encrypted: access them only via "pencil" MCP tools — never use Read or Grep on .pen files. Follow each tool's input schema exactly.

Always call get_editor_state(include_schema: true) if you don't have the current .pen file schema in the context. Knowing the schema is required to use any other Pencil MCP tool.

- cora-email: Use when the user asks about their email, inbox, briefs, email todos, or wants to interact with Cora — an AI email assistant. Covers checking briefs, managing todos, drafting replies, and chatting with Cora via the CLI.
- impeccable: Use when the user wants to design, redesign, shape, critique, audit, polish, clarify, distill, harden, optimize, adapt, animate, colorize, extract, or otherwise improve a frontend interface. Covers websites, landing pages, dashboards, product UI, app shells, components, forms, settings, onboarding, and empty states. Handles UX review, visual hierarchy, information architecture, cognitive load, accessibility, performance, responsive behavior, theming, anti-patterns, typography, fonts, spacing, layout, alignment, color, motion, micro-interactions, UX copy, error states, edge cases, i18n, and reusable design systems or tokens. Also use for bland designs that need to become bolder or more delightful, loud designs that should become quieter, live browser iteration on UI elements, or ambitious visual effects that should feel technically extraordinary. Not for backend-only or non-UI tasks.
- plane-time-tracking: Automatically tracks Claude Code session time to Plane project management issues. This skill MUST be activated at the very start of every Claude Code session — before doing any other work. It reads a `.plane` config from ~/.claude/.plane, parses the current git branch to extract the issue identifier (e.g., PROJ-123), starts a timer via the Plane API, and stops it when the session ends. If the branch doesn't follow the naming convention or the user is on main/master, the skill asks the user which issue to track to. Use this skill whenever: a session begins in a repo with a git branch, the user mentions time tracking, the user says "start timer" or "stop timer", or the user asks about logging work to Plane.
- plus-one-ssh: Use when the user asks to inspect, update, fix, or customize a Plus One bot over SSH.
- react-nextjs: React 19 + Next.js 15 code patterns and examples for TypeScript. Covers Server/Client Components, App Router conventions, React 19 features (use hook, actions, useOptimistic, compiler), TanStack Query, form handling with Zod, Zustand, error boundaries, and performance patterns. Use when building React components, pages, or Next.js features. Complements CLAUDE.md — does not repeat architecture, file structure, or TypeScript style rules already defined there.
- shipping-a-plan: Use when an implementation plan or approved spec exists and the goal is to take it from "plan written" to "merged on main with local cleaned up." Triggers on phrases like "ship this plan", "run plan N end-to-end", "execute and merge", or any time the next step is mechanical orchestration of execute → PR → review → resolve → merge → cleanup rather than fresh design work.
- use-spark: Use the spark CLI to access the user's Spark email data - list emails, search by topic, read threads, check calendar events, find availability, look up contacts, and view team info. Use when the user asks about their emails, calendar, contacts, meetings, or scheduling.
- start-timer: Start Time Tracking
- stop-timer: Stop Time Tracking
- tracking-status: Time Tracking Status
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- kb:extract: Extract wiki-worthy knowledge from unprocessed session manifests
- kb:ingest: Ingest a source (file, URL, or pasted text) into the KB vault
- kb:lint: Lint the KB vault — orphans, dead links, stale content, contradictions
- kb:query: Query the KB vault and synthesize an answer from wiki pages
- kb:refine: Refine the KB vault — stale pages, weak connections, merges, backlinks
- pr-review-toolkit:review-pr: Comprehensive PR review using specialized agents
- compound-engineering:ce-agent-native-architecture: Build applications where agents are first-class citizens. Use this skill when designing autonomous agents, creating MCP tools, implementing self-modifying systems, or building apps where features are outcomes achieved by agents operating in a loop.
- compound-engineering:ce-brainstorm: Explore requirements and approaches through collaborative dialogue, then write a right-sized requirements document. Use when the user says "let's brainstorm", "what should we build", or "help me think through X", presents a vague or ambitious feature request, or seems unsure about scope or direction -- even without explicitly asking to brainstorm.
- compound-engineering:ce-clean-gone-branches: Clean up local branches whose remote tracking branch is gone. Use when the user says "clean up branches", "delete gone branches", "prune local branches", "clean gone", or wants to remove stale local branches that no longer exist on the remote. Also handles removing associated worktrees for branches that have them.
- compound-engineering:ce-code-review: Structured code review using tiered persona agents, confidence-gated findings, and a merge/dedup pipeline. Use when reviewing code changes before creating a PR.
- compound-engineering:ce-commit: Create a git commit with a clear, value-communicating message. Use when the user says "commit", "commit this", "save my changes", "create a commit", or wants to commit staged or unstaged work. Produces well-structured commit messages that follow repo conventions when they exist, and defaults to conventional commit format otherwise.
- compound-engineering:ce-commit-push-pr: Commit, push, and open a PR with an adaptive, value-first description that scales in depth with the change. Use when the user says "commit and PR", "ship this", "create a PR", or "open a pull request". Also handles description-only flows ("write a PR description", "rewrite the PR body", "describe this PR") without committing or pushing.
- compound-engineering:ce-compound: Document a recently solved problem to compound your team's knowledge
- compound-engineering:ce-compound-refresh: Refresh stale learning and pattern docs under docs/solutions/ by reviewing them against the current codebase, then updating, consolidating, or deleting drifted ones. Use when the user asks to "refresh my learnings", "audit docs/solutions/", "clean up stale learnings", or "consolidate overlapping docs", or when ce-compound flags an older doc as superseded. Do not trigger for general refactor, debugging, or code-review work unless the user has explicitly pointed at docs/solutions/.
- compound-engineering:ce-debug: Systematically find root causes and fix bugs. Use when debugging errors, investigating test failures, reproducing bugs from issue trackers (GitHub, Linear, Jira), or when stuck on a problem after failed fix attempts. Also use when the user says 'debug this', 'why is this failing', 'fix this bug', 'trace this error', or pastes stack traces, error messages, or issue references.
- compound-engineering:ce-demo-reel: Capture a visual demo reel (GIF, terminal recording, screenshots) for PR descriptions. Use when shipping UI changes, CLI features, or any work with observable behavior that benefits from visual proof. Also use when asked to add a demo, record a GIF, screenshot a feature, show what changed visually, create a demo reel, capture evidence, add proof to a PR, or create a before/after comparison.
- compound-engineering:ce-dhh-rails-style: This skill should be used when writing Ruby and Rails code in DHH's distinctive 37signals style. It applies when writing Ruby code, Rails applications, creating models, controllers, or any Ruby file. Triggers on Ruby/Rails code generation, refactoring requests, code review, or when the user mentions DHH, 37signals, Basecamp, HEY, or Campfire style. Embodies REST purity, fat models, thin controllers, Current attributes, Hotwire patterns, and the "clarity over cleverness" philosophy.
- compound-engineering:ce-doc-review: Review requirements or plan documents using parallel persona agents that surface role-specific issues. Use when a requirements document or plan document exists and the user wants to improve it.
- compound-engineering:ce-frontend-design: Build web interfaces with genuine design quality, not AI slop. Use for any frontend work - landing pages, web apps, dashboards, admin panels, components, interactive experiences. Activates for both greenfield builds and modifications to existing applications. Detects existing design systems and respects them. Covers composition, typography, color, motion, and copy. Verifies results via screenshots before declaring done.
- compound-engineering:ce-gemini-imagegen: This skill should be used when generating and editing images using the Gemini API (Nano Banana Pro). It applies when creating images from text prompts, editing existing images, applying style transfers, generating logos with text, creating stickers, product mockups, or any image generation/manipulation task. Supports text-to-image, image editing, multi-turn refinement, and composition from multiple reference images.
- compound-engineering:ce-ideate: Generate and critically evaluate grounded ideas about a topic. Use when asking what to improve, requesting idea generation, exploring surprising directions, or wanting the AI to proactively suggest strong options before brainstorming one in depth. Triggers on phrases like 'what should I improve', 'give me ideas', 'ideate on X', 'surprise me', 'what would you change', or any request for AI-generated suggestions rather than refining the user's own idea.
- compound-engineering:ce-optimize: Run metric-driven iterative optimization loops -- define a measurable goal, run parallel experiments, measure each against hard gates or LLM-as-judge scores, keep improvements, and converge on the best solution. Use when optimizing clustering quality, search relevance, build performance, prompt quality, or any measurable outcome that benefits from systematic experimentation.
- compound-engineering:ce-plan: Create structured plans for multi-step tasks -- software features, research workflows, events, study plans, or any goal that benefits from breakdown. Also deepens existing plans with interactive sub-agent review. Use when the user says 'plan this', 'create a plan', 'how should we build', 'break this down', or when a brainstorm doc is ready for planning. Use 'deepen the plan' or 'deepening pass' for the deepening flow. For exploratory requests, prefer ce-brainstorm first.
- compound-engineering:ce-product-pulse: Generate a time-windowed pulse report on what users experienced and how the product performed - usage, quality, errors, signals worth investigating. Use when the user says 'run a pulse', 'show me the pulse', 'how are we doing', 'weekly recap', 'launch-day check', or passes a time window like '24h' or '7d'. - compound-engineering:ce-proof: Run human-in-the-loop review loops over markdown via Proof (proofeditor.ai) — share, view, comment on, edit, and sync collaborative docs. Use when the user says "view this in proof", "share to proof", "HITL this doc", or wants a shared markdown review surface for a spec, plan, or draft, including handoffs from ce-brainstorm, ce-ideate, or ce-plan. Do not trigger on "proof" meaning evidence, math proofs, proof-of-concept, or "proofread this".
- compound-engineering:ce-resolve-pr-feedback: Resolve PR review feedback by evaluating validity and fixing issues in parallel. Use when addressing PR review comments, resolving review threads, or fixing code review feedback.
- compound-engineering:ce-riffrec-feedback-analysis: Riffrec product-feedback workflow. ALWAYS load when the user posts a `riffrec-*.zip`, a bundle with `session.json` + `events.json` + `recording.webm` + `voice.webm`, a video/audio recording for product feedback, or asks how to capture and share Riffrec sessions. Routes between setup, quick bug report, and extensive analysis.
- compound-engineering:ce-sessions: Search and ask questions about coding agent session history across Claude Code, Codex, and Cursor. Use when asking what was worked on, what was tried before, how a problem was investigated across sessions, what happened recently, or any question about past agent sessions. Also use when the user references prior sessions, previous attempts, or past investigations — even without saying 'sessions' explicitly.
- compound-engineering:ce-simplify-code: Simplify and refine recently changed code for clarity, reuse, quality, and efficiency while preserving behavior.
- compound-engineering:ce-slack-research: Search Slack for interpreted organizational context -- decisions, constraints, and discussion arcs -- and produce a synthesized research digest with cross-cutting analysis. Use when the user says 'search slack for', 'what did we discuss about', 'slack context for', or 'what does the team think about'. Differs from slack:find-discussions, which returns raw message results without synthesis.
- compound-engineering:ce-strategy: Create or maintain STRATEGY.md - the product's target problem, approach, users, key metrics, and tracks of work. - compound-engineering:ce-test-browser: Run browser tests on pages affected by current PR or branch
- compound-engineering:ce-work: Execute work efficiently while maintaining quality and finishing features
- compound-engineering:ce-worktree: Create an isolated git worktree for parallel feature work or PR review. Use when starting work that should not disturb the current checkout, or when `ce-work` or `ce-code-review` offers a worktree option.

Use only when the user explicitly requests hands-off execution of a software task and provides a feature description; do not auto-route casual conversation here.
- coding-tutor:coding-tutor: Personalized coding tutorials that build on your existing knowledge and use your actual codebase for examples. Creates a persistent learning trail that compounds over time using the power of AI, spaced repetition and quizes.
- kb:extract: Extract wiki-worthy knowledge from unprocessed session manifests. Sessions are sources — extraction runs the normal ingest workflow.
- kb:kb: Persistent memory vault for Claude Code. Teaches ingest, query, and lint workflows for a markdown-based knowledge vault using Obsidian-flavored markdown.
- kb:refine: Systematic vault refinement — find stale pages, weak connections, merge/split candidates, and backlink gaps. Interactive, user-approved.

Use when completing features, making design decisions, or when user asks to understand code better. Supports the user's stated goal of understanding design choices as learning opportunities.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

lastPrompt: LEts add support for Antrigravity now, do research and understand the latest api (I think this is v2), but make sure we're using the accurate docs

aiTitle: Add Antigravity support with v2 API

content.text:
I'll research this properly. Let me start by understanding the existing adapter architecture in `agent-sync` and researching the latest Antigravity docs in parallel.

First, let me load the web tools I'll need and kick off codebase exploration concurrently.

input.prompt:
I'm working in the agent-sync Go project at /Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync. I need to understand the adapter/target architecture so I can add a new adapter for "Antigravity CLI" (Google's Antigravity agentic IDE/CLI).

Please investigate and report back with concrete file paths and code excerpts:

1. Where are the existing tool adapters/targets defined? (e.g. Claude → CLAUDE.md, Cursor, Gemini/Antigravity → GEMINI.md). List each adapter, the file it lives in, and the interface/struct it implements.
2. What is the common interface an adapter must implement? Show the interface definition and method signatures.
3. How does an adapter declare: the output file path(s) it writes, the scope-awareness (project vs user/home), which "kinds" of content it supports (agents-md, skills, commands, etc.), and how it renders content?
4. How are adapters registered (a registry, a slice, an init function)? Show the registration site.
5. What did commit #36 (b91321a is #38... actually #36 is 4987ca0 "chore: Gemini CLI → Antigravity CLI") change? Run `git show 4987ca0 --stat` and summarize which files were touched and what the "GEMINI.md overlay retarget" means — is there already a partial Antigravity target, or is it still named Gemini internally?
6. Where is the roadmap/plan doc that mentions the Antigravity CLI adapter? Find docs/plans/* and docs/roadmap references and quote the relevant Antigravity section.

I now have a complete picture of the architecture. Here is my report.

---

# Adapter/Target Architecture — agent-sync

## Overview: two-layer design

agent-sync separates **canonical IR** (tool-agnostic intermediate representation, decoded from a canonical repo) from **adapters** that translate IR into each tool's native on-disk layout. Adapters run as an *out-of-process JSON-RPC subprocess* OR as *in-process "bundled" adapters* driven over a pipe. The runtime drives a four-phase lifecycle: `initialize → initialized → emit → shutdown`. Critically, **adapters emit ops describing writes; the CLI core performs the actual filesystem writes** (adapters do zero file I/O).

---

## 1. Existing adapters — files and what they emit

All bundled adapters live under `/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/adapter/bundled/<name>/`. There are **four** bundled today (`claude`, `cursor`, `codex`, `pi`).

| Adapter | Package dir | Entry file | Emits |
|---|---|---|---|
| Claude Code | `bundled/claude/` | `bundled.go:41` `Bundled()` | `CLAUDE.md` (agents-md overlay), `.claude/rules/agent-sync/`, `.claude/commands/agent-sync/`, `.claude/skills/agent-sync-<id>/`, `.mcp.json` |
| Cursor | `bundled/cursor/` | `bundled.go:42` `Bundled()` | `.cursor/rules`, AGENTS.md, mcp |
| Codex CLI | `bundled/codex/` | `bundled.go:51` `Bundled()` | `AGENTS.md` (managed section), `.agents/skills/`, mcp (`~/.codex/...` at user scope) |
| Pi | `bundled/pi/` | `bundled.go:54` `Bundled()` | `AGENTS.md` overlay, `.agents/skills/agent-sync-<id>/`; rule/command/mcp/plugin = unsupported warnings |

There is **no adapter named `gemini` or `antigravity`** — neither has ever shipped. See section 5.

Each adapter is not a Go interface implementation in the classic sense — it returns an `*adapter.BundledAdapter` struct (defined in `internal/adapter/discover.go`) whose `Run` field is an in-process entry point that starts an `adapterkit.Server`. The lifecycle contract is a JSON-RPC protocol, not a Go interface.

---

## 2. The contract an adapter must satisfy

The "interface" is the **JSON-RPC protocol** implemented via the SDK `pkg/adapterkit`. An adapter's `run` function (e.g. `bundled/claude/bundled.go:64-108`) constructs an `adapterkit.Server` and registers two handlers:

```go
server.OnInitialize(func(_ context.Context, params adapterkit.InitializeParams) (adapterkit.InitializeResult, error) {
    scope = params.Scope
    sourceURL = params.SourceURL
    sourceCommit = params.SourceCommit
    return adapterkit.InitializeResult{
        Capabilities:    capabilitiesForWire(),
        DeclaredOutputs: declaredOutputs(scope),
    }, nil
})
server.OnEmit(func(ctx context.Context, params adapterkit.EmitParams) (adapterkit.EmitResult, error) {
    return handleEmit(ctx, params, scope, sourceURL, sourceCommit)
})
```

Key SDK types (`/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/pkg/adapterkit/`):
- `server.go:97` `NewServer(ServerOptions)`, `:127` `OnInitialize`, `:134` `OnEmit`
- `capabilities.go` — `NewCapabilities()`, `.Supports(kind)`, `.Partial(kind)`, `.Unsupported(kind)`, `.WithWriteToolOwned(bool)`, `.Build()`
- `types.go:159` `DeclaredOutput`, `types.go:192` `Op` interface, `types.go:41-51` `OutputMode`
- `pkg/adapterkit/doc.go` has a minimal worked example ("echo" adapter) — the canonical starting template.

The `BundledAdapter` struct + `AdapterManifest` are defined in `internal/adapter/discover.go` (Manifest fields: `Name`, `Version`, `ContractVersion`, `ReservedPrefix`, `Command`; plus `Run`).

The session lifecycle machinery (not something a new adapter re-implements) is in `internal/adapter/adapter.go`, `runtime.go`, `inproc.go`, and `contract/protocol.go`.

---

## 3. How an adapter declares its behavior

Every bundled adapter follows the same five-file layout. Using `claude` as the reference:

**(a) Output paths — `declaredOutputs(scope)`** in `capabilities.go:73-102`:
```go
outs := []adapterkit.DeclaredOutput{
    {Path: ".claude/rules/agent-sync",   Mode: adapterkit.OutputModeOwnedSubdir},
    {Path: ".claude/commands/agent-sync", Mode: adapterkit.OutputModeOwnedSubdir},
    {Path: ".claude/skills",              Mode: adapterkit.OutputModeSharedSubdir},
    {Path: paths.mcpJSON,  Mode: adapterkit.OutputModeToolOwnedEntry, JSONPointer: &mcpPointer},
    {Path: paths.claudeMD, Mode: adapterkit.OutputModeToolOwnedEntry, SectionID: &claudeMDSection},
}
```
`OutputMode` values (`pkg/adapterkit/types.go:41-51`): `owned-subdir` (swapped wholesale), `shared-subdir` (manage only our leaf dirs), `tool-owned-entry` (merge into a user-owned file via JSON pointer or markdown section). The runtime's path-safety gate rejects any emitted op whose path isn't inside a declared output.

**(b) Scope-awareness (project vs user/home)** — `emit_tool_owned.go:31-46`:
```go
func resolvePathSet(scope string) pathSet {
    if scope == scopeUser {
        return pathSet{claudeMD: ".claude/CLAUDE.md", mcpJSON: ".claude.json", sidecar: ""}
    }
    return pathSet{claudeMD: "CLAUDE.md", mcpJSON: ".mcp.json", sidecar: ".agent-sync-managed"}
}
```
Scope arrives in `InitializeParams.Scope` (`"user"`/`"project"`/`"directory"`; empty ⇒ project). Both `declaredOutputs` and the emit paths MUST resolve from this one function so declared and emitted paths never drift.

**(c) Supported kinds** — declared twice and kept in parity: the embedded `capabilities.yaml` and the in-code `conceptKinds` map in `capabilities.go:40-47`:
```go
var conceptKinds = map[ir.Kind]capmatrix.CapabilityStatus{
    ir.KindAgentsMD:        capmatrix.Supported,
    ir.KindRule:            capmatrix.Supported,
    ir.KindSkill:           capmatrix.Supported,
    ir.KindCommand:         capmatrix.Supported,
    ir.KindMCPServerEntry:  capmatrix.Supported,
    ir.KindPluginReference: capmatrix.Unsupported,
}
```
A parity test enforces the yaml↔code match (`capabilities_test.go` `TestCapabilitiesYAML_MatchesCodeMap`). IR kinds are enumerated in `internal/ir/kinds.go`.

**(d) Rendering** — `emit.go` (`handleEmit` at `:99`, `dispatchNode` switch at `:214-240`) routes each node by kind to a per-kind emitter. Content-producing emitters live in `emit.go`, `emit_tool_owned.go`, `emit_reserved.go`; managed-file headers/markers in `header.go`. Adapters return `adapterkit.Op` values (e.g. `OpWriteFile`, `OpWriteToolOwned`, `OpWarning`, `OpMkdir`) — never write to disk.

---

## 4. Registration

Single registration site: `/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/cli/setup.go:22-29`:
```go
func bundledAdapters() []*adapter.BundledAdapter {
    return []*adapter.BundledAdapter{
        claudeadapter.Bundled(),
        cursoradapter.Bundled(),
        codexadapter.Bundled(),
        piadapter.Bundled(),
    }
}
```
This slice is passed into `adapter.DiscoverAdapters(...)` at `setup.go:114-117` via `DiscoverOptions.Bundled`. Discovery (`internal/adapter/discover.go:210-211`) wraps each as `Source: SourceBundled`. **To add Antigravity you add one line here plus a new import** (`antigravityadapter "…/bundled/antigravity"`).

Additionally, a static per-target native-read table lives in `internal/coverage/coverage.go:45-69` (`nativeAtDirectory`, `nonNativeAtUser`). Unknown targets default to "fully native / no warnings" (`known()` at `:73`), so a new adapter works without editing this — but for accurate directory/user-scope coverage warnings you'd add an `antigravity` entry.

---

## 5. Commit `4987ca0` (PR #36) — "Gemini CLI → Antigravity CLI"

`git show 4987ca0 --stat` touched **6 files** (37 insertions, 12 deletions):
- `CHANGELOG.md`
- `README.md` — planned-adapter table swaps "Gemini CLI" → "Antigravity CLI (replaces Gemini CLI, retired 2026-06-18) | Planned (supported)" and fixes the bundled count to "Four" (`README.md:91`).
- `docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md` — dated divergence note.
- `docs/spec/ir-v1.md` — spec text for the overlay retarget.
- `internal/ir/decode.go` — **the actual behavior change**.
- `internal/ir/decode_test.go`.

**What "GEMINI.md overlay retarget" means:** The only code change is in `internal/ir/decode.go:26-30`, the `rootAgentsMDOverlay` map:
```go
var rootAgentsMDOverlay = map[string]string{
    "CLAUDE.md": "claude",
    "GEMINI.md": "antigravity",   // was "gemini"
}
```
When the IR decoder finds a root `GEMINI.md`, it produces an `agents-md` node whose **node id stays `gemini`** (filename-derived) but whose **target scope is now `antigravity`** instead of the never-shipped `gemini`.

**Answer to your question:** There is **no partial Antigravity adapter** — no `bundled/antigravity/` package, no registration, no capabilities. The only "Antigravity" presence is (1) this overlay-target string in the decoder, (2) the coverage/README/spec/plan docs. The rename is internal-target-only: overlays authored today (`GEMINI.md`) will apply once you build and register the Antigravity adapter. Nothing is "still named Gemini internally" in a way that blocks you — the target string is already `antigravity`.

---

## 6. Roadmap / plan doc for the Antigravity adapter

Authoritative living plan: **`/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md:57`**:

> "Supported-tier `antigravity` adapter and experimental-tier `windsurf`, `lmstudio`, `roo` adapters: separate PRs after primary adapters land. … **Divergence (resolved, 2026-07-02):** the origin doc (R3) names the supported-tier target `gemini`, but Google retired Gemini CLI on 2026-06-18 in favor of Antigravity CLI, which reads the same `GEMINI.md` overlay alongside `AGENTS.md`. The deferred supported-tier adapter is therefore `antigravity`. The `GEMINI.md` root overlay keeps its filename-derived node id `gemini` but scopes to the `antigravity` target (target renamed `gemini` → `antigravity` in PR #36; spec updated in `docs/spec/ir-v1.md`)."

Spec detail: `docs/spec/ir-v1.md:28` and `:79-89` describe the overlay/target semantics. Other plan/handoff references to the (now-antigravity) supported-tier adapter as future work: `docs/handoffs/2026-07-01-001-hierarchy-composition-handoff.md:139`, `docs/plans/2026-06-15-001-fix-dogfooding-readiness-plan.md:24,98`. Note the origin brainstorm (`docs/brainstorms/2026-04-21-aienvs-agent-workspace-requirements.md` R3/R11) still says `gemini` — intentionally left as a historical record.

There is no per-adapter design doc for Antigravity yet; existing ones are `docs/adapters/{claude,codex,cursor,pi}.md` — you'd add `docs/adapters/antigravity.md`.

---

## 7. Testing pattern

Each adapter ships a mirror set of tests plus JSON IR fixtures. For `claude`:
- `bundled/claude/emit_test.go` — the core pattern. `emitFixture(t, name)` (`:20-38`) reads a JSON IR doc from `testdata/ir/<name>.json`, runs the real `handleEmit`/`captureOps` path (same sort+dedup as the wire path), and returns `(EmitResult, []Op)`. Tests assert on `res.OpsPerformed` (the `{Op, Path}` `OpRecord` summary) and inspect individual op fields:
```go
res, ops := emitFixture(t, "rule-only.json")
wantRecords := []adapterkit.OpRecord{
    {Op: adapterkit.OpKindMkdir,     Path: ".claude/rules/agent-sync"},
    {Op: adapterkit.OpKindWriteFile, Path: ".claude/rules/agent-sync/README.md"},
    {Op: adapterkit.OpKindWriteFile, Path: ".claude/rules/agent-sync/no-fri.md"},
}
```
- **Fixtures** = input-only JSON IR docs in `bundled/claude/testdata/ir/` (e.g. `rule-only.json`, `skill-with-assets.json`, `mixed-everything.json`, `plugin-reference-warn.json`, `targeted-other.json`). There are **no golden output files** — expected ops are inlined as Go literals (`wantRecords`), and expected byte content is asserted via helpers like `findWriteFile`.
- `capabilities_test.go` — `TestCapabilitiesYAML_MatchesCodeMap` (yaml↔code parity) and `TestCapabilitiesForWire_ExposesAllKinds`.
- `header_test.go`, `bundled_test.go`, `emit_reserved`/`emit_tool_owned`/`emit_opcontent` tests.

Cross-adapter conformance harnesses also exist: `internal/adapter/conformance/` and `internal/adapter/contract/` (protocol-level), plus `internal/adapter/runtime_test.go`.

---

## Recommended template for the new adapter

**`pi`** (`internal/adapter/bundled/pi/`) is the closest starting point for Antigravity: it is the newest, is agents-md-centric (Antigravity's primary concern is the `GEMINI.md`/`AGENTS.md` overlay), owns no dedicated reserved subdir, and cleanly demonstrates unsupported-kind warnings and scope-aware `resolvePathSet`. Copy pi's file structure (`bundled.go`, `capabilities.go` + `capabilities.yaml`, `emit*.go`, `header.go`, tests + `testdata/ir/`), set `adapterName = "antigravity"`, then register it in `internal/cli/setup.go:22`.

**Files you'll create/touch:** new `internal/adapter/bundled/antigravity/` package; one import + one line in `internal/cli/setup.go`; optionally `internal/coverage/coverage.go` (native-read table) and `docs/adapters/antigravity.md`. The IR-side wiring (`GEMINI.md` → `antigravity` target) is already done by PR #36.

Other plan/handoff references to the (now-antigravity) supported-tier adapter as future work: `docs/handoffs/2026-07-01-001-hierarchy-composition-handoff.md:139`, `docs/plans/2026-06-15-001-fix-dogfooding-readiness-plan.md:24,98`. Note the origin brainstorm (`docs/brainstorms/2026-04-21-aienvs-agent-workspace-requirements.md` R3/R11) still says `gemini` — intentionally left as a historical record.

There is no per-adapter design doc for Antigravity yet; existing ones are `docs/adapters/{claude,codex,cursor,pi}.md` — you'd add `docs/adapters/antigravity.md`.

---

## 7. Testing pattern

Each adapter ships a mirror set of tests plus JSON IR fixtures. For `claude`:
- `bundled/claude/emit_test.go` — the core pattern. `emitFixture(t, name)` (`:20-38`) reads a JSON IR doc from `testdata/ir/<name>.json`, runs the real `handleEmit`/`captureOps` path (same sort+dedup as the wire path), and returns `(EmitResult, []Op)`. Tests assert on `res.OpsPerformed` (the `{Op, Path}` `OpRecord` summary) and inspect individual op fields:
```go
res, ops := emitFixture(t, "rule-only.json")
wantRecords := []adapterkit.OpRecord{
    {Op: adapterkit.OpKindMkdir,     Path: ".claude/rules/agent-sync"},
    {Op: adapterkit.OpKindWriteFile, Path: ".claude/rules/agent-sync/README.md"},
    {Op: adapterkit.OpKindWriteFile, Path: ".claude/rules/agent-sync/no-fri.md"},
}
```
- **Fixtures** = input-only JSON IR docs in `bundled/claude/testdata/ir/` (e.g. `rule-only.json`, `skill-with-assets.json`, `mixed-everything.json`, `plugin-reference-warn.json`, `targeted-other.json`). There are **no golden output files** — expected ops are inlined as Go literals (`wantRecords`), and expected byte content is asserted via helpers like `findWriteFile`.
- `capabilities_test.go` — `TestCapabilitiesYAML_MatchesCodeMap` (yaml↔code parity) and `TestCapabilitiesForWire_ExposesAllKinds`.
- `header_test.go`, `bundled_test.go`, `emit_reserved`/`emit_tool_owned`/`emit_opcontent` tests.

Cross-adapter conformance harnesses also exist: `internal/adapter/conformance/` and `internal/adapter/contract/` (protocol-level), plus `internal/adapter/runtime_test.go`.

---

## Recommended template for the new adapter

**`pi`** (`internal/adapter/bundled/pi/`) is the closest starting point for Antigravity: it is the newest, is agents-md-centric (Antigravity's primary concern is the `GEMINI.md`/`AGENTS.md` overlay), owns no dedicated reserved subdir, and cleanly demonstrates unsupported-kind warnings and scope-aware `resolvePathSet`. Copy pi's file structure (`bundled.go`, `capabilities.go` + `capabilities.yaml`, `emit*.go`, `header.go`, tests + `testdata/ir/`), set `adapterName = "antigravity"`, then register it in `internal/cli/setup.go:22`.

**Files you'll create/touch:** new `internal/adapter/bundled/antigravity/` package; one import + one line in `internal/cli/setup.go`; optionally `internal/coverage/coverage.go` (native-read table) and `docs/adapters/antigravity.md`. The IR-side wiring (`GEMINI.md` → `antigravity` target) is already done by PR #36.

Based on the search results, here's information about Google Antigravity IDE agent context files, AGENTS.md, GEMINI.md, and rules configuration:

## Configuration Files Overview

The agent parses and enforces rule constraints defined inside your active directory's GEMINI.md and AGENTS.md files, while the agent automatically consults and enforces your global constraints located at ~/.gemini/GEMINI.md.

## AGENTS.md vs GEMINI.md

AGENTS.md is a cross-platform rules file that works across multiple AI coding tools. Instead of maintaining separate rules files for each tool, you write one AGENTS.md and every compatible tool reads it.

Starting with Antigravity v1.20.3 (March 5, 2026), the IDE reads rules from AGENTS.md in addition to GEMINI.md.

## Priority Hierarchy

When both files exist, GEMINI.md has the highest priority as Antigravity-specific rules that override everything below them. AGENTS.md is cross-tool foundation rules read by Antigravity, Cursor, and Claude Code, applied after GEMINI.md, so conflicting rules defer to GEMINI.md.

## Agent Configuration

The agent supports a filesystem-native approach to customization: you can mount files like AGENTS.md for instructions and skills under .agents/skills/ directly into the sandbox.

Workspace rules can be organized using .agent/rules/ for organizing multiple workspace rules into separate files.

Based on the web search results, here's the information about Google Antigravity MCP server configuration:

## MCP Configuration File Locations

Antigravity 2.0, IDE, and CLI share a central MCP configuration in the file ~/.gemini/config/mcp_config.json. Additionally, the configuration file is located globally at ~/.gemini/config/mcp_config.json (or locally in your workspace under .agents/mcp_config.json).

## Accessing the Configuration

There are several ways to access and modify the `mcp_config.json` file:

1. **Through the GUI:** Go to the Antigravity Settings and click on the Customizations Tab, then click on the Open MCP Config button to open the mcp_config.json file in the editor.

2. **Via MCP Store:** Click on "Manage MCP Servers", then click on "View raw config" to open the mcp_config.json file.

3. **Using CLI:** Type /mcp inside the prompt panel and press Enter to open the interactive MCP Manager Overlay.

## Configuration Format

The configuration file follows a standardized format with a single mcpServers object where you define each server you want to connect to.

The configuration automatically reloads when saved, so changes take effect immediately without requiring a restart.
