# agent-sync

[![Release](https://img.shields.io/github/v/release/JustinBeaudry/agent-sync?label=release)](https://github.com/JustinBeaudry/agent-sync/releases/latest)

`agent-sync` is a Go CLI that keeps AI-agent configuration for multiple tools
(Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI — and more to come) in sync from a single
Git-backed manifest. Think of it as `dotfiles` for agents: one canonical
source of skills, rules, commands, and MCP servers; native files rendered
into each tool's own conventions.

> **Status:** pre-alpha. v1 is in active development. Architecture and
> rationale live in the [requirements](docs/brainstorms/2026-04-21-aienvs-agent-workspace-requirements.md)
> and [implementation plan](docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md).

## Install

Download the prebuilt binary for your platform, verify its checksum, and put it
on your PATH — one command:

```bash
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JustinBeaudry/agent-sync/main/install.sh | sh
```

Installs to `/usr/local/bin` (falling back to `~/.local/bin` if that isn't
writable). Pin a version or change the location with env vars:

```bash
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JustinBeaudry/agent-sync/main/install.sh \
  | AGENT_SYNC_VERSION=v0.1.0 AGENT_SYNC_INSTALL_DIR="$HOME/bin" sh
```

Prefer not to pipe to a shell? Download the archive for your OS/arch from the
[Releases page](https://github.com/JustinBeaudry/agent-sync/releases), verify it
against `checksums.txt`, extract the `agent-sync` binary, and move it onto your
PATH. (Windows: grab the `.zip`.) Git must be on your PATH at runtime.

## Quickstart

New to agent-sync? [`docs/quickstart.md`](docs/quickstart.md) walks through
authoring a canonical repo and running your first `init → sync → validate`,
with a copyable example under [`examples/canonical/`](examples/canonical/).

## What agent-sync does

1. **Manifests form a hierarchy.** A `.agent-sync.yaml` binds a directory to a
   canonical source and declares which target tools receive configuration. The
   source is one of: a remote Git repo (`url`) or a local clone (`local_path`),
   both pinned by commit SHA; or an in-repo working-tree directory (`local_dir`,
   e.g. `.agents`) for per-repo skills authored right in the repo — unpinned,
   and exempt from trust and offline-strict since there's nothing remote to fetch.
   `sync` walks up from the current directory (project root = nearest `.git`
   ancestor) and emits every manifest it finds, each to its own scope; the
   user-level manifest at `~` is emitted only with `sync --user`. agent-sync
   never merges across levels — each target tool resolves precedence via its own
   native config hierarchy, and `sync` warns when a scope emits a kind a tool
   won't read natively at that level.
2. **Explicit `sync`.** `agent-sync sync` materializes the pinned content,
   compiles it via per-tool adapters, stages the output, and atomically
   swaps it into each tool's reserved subdirectory (e.g.
   `.claude/rules/agent-sync/`, `.cursor/rules/agent-sync/`, `.codex/skills/agent-sync-<id>/`).
   Emitted skills carry a real `name` / `description` frontmatter block so
   they show up correctly in each tool's skill list, and every managed file
   records its source (`<url>@<short-sha>`) in its header.
3. **Moving the pin: `update`.** `agent-sync update` fetches the canonical
   remote, shows what changed since the current pin, and — on confirmation
   (or `--accept-update=<sha>` when non-interactive) — re-pins
   `commit` + `trusted_sha` and re-syncs in one locked step. It is
   fast-forward-only: a rewritten upstream history is refused unless you
   pass `--accept-rewritten-history=<sha>`. Use `--user` to update the
   `~` manifest.
4. **Safe by default.** Pinning, offline-strict, TOFU on `(URL, SHA)`
   pairs, reserved-prefix ownership with a ledger, and atomic swap with
   rollback — designed to fail closed.
5. **Capability-honest translation.** A tool-agnostic IR (`agents-md`,

# agent-sync

[![Release](https://img.shields.io/github/v/release/JustinBeaudry/agent-sync?label=release)](https://github.com/JustinBeaudry/agent-sync/releases/latest)

`agent-sync` is a Go CLI that keeps AI-agent configuration for multiple tools
(Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI — and more to come) in sync from a single
Git-backed manifest. Think of it as `dotfiles` for agents: one canonical
source of skills, rules, commands, and MCP servers; native files rendered
into each tool's own conventions.

> **Status:** pre-alpha. v1 is in active development. Architecture and
> rationale live in the [requirements](docs/brainstorms/2026-04-21-aienvs-agent-workspace-requirements.md)
> and [implementation plan](docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md).

## Install

Download the prebuilt binary for your platform, verify its checksum, and put it
on your PATH — one command:

```bash
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JustinBeaudry/agent-sync/main/install.sh | sh
```

Installs to `/usr/local/bin` (falling back to `~/.local/bin` if that isn't
writable). Pin a version or change the location with env vars:

```bash
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JustinBeaudry/agent-sync/main/install.sh \
  | AGENT_SYNC_VERSION=v0.1.0 AGENT_SYNC_INSTALL_DIR="$HOME/bin" sh
```

Prefer not to pipe to a shell? Download the archive for your OS/arch from the
[Releases page](https://github.com/JustinBeaudry/agent-sync/releases), verify it
against `checksums.txt`, extract the `agent-sync` binary, and move it onto your
PATH. (Windows: grab the `.zip`.) Git must be on your PATH at runtime.

## Quickstart

New to agent-sync? [`docs/quickstart.md`](docs/quickstart.md) walks through
authoring a canonical repo and running your first `init → sync → validate`,
with a copyable example under [`examples/canonical/`](examples/canonical/).

## What agent-sync does

1. **Manifests form a hierarchy.** A `.agent-sync.yaml` binds a directory to a
   canonical source and declares which target tools receive configuration. The
   source is one of: a remote Git repo (`url`) or a local clone (`local_path`),
   both pinned by commit SHA; or an in-repo working-tree directory (`local_dir`,
   e.g. `.agents`) for per-repo skills authored right in the repo — unpinned,
   and exempt from trust and offline-strict since there's nothing remote to fetch.
   `sync` walks up from the current directory (project root = nearest `.git`
   ancestor) and emits every manifest it finds, each to its own scope; the
   user-level manifest at `~` is emitted only with `sync --user`. agent-sync
   never merges across levels — each target tool resolves precedence via its own
   native config hierarchy, and `sync` warns when a scope emits a kind a tool
   won't read natively at that level.
2. **Explicit `sync`.** `agent-sync sync` materializes the pinned content,
   compiles it via per-tool adapters, stages the output, and atomically
   swaps it into each tool's reserved subdirectory (e.g.
   `.claude/rules/agent-sync/`, `.cursor/rules/agent-sync/`, `.codex/skills/agent-sync-<id>/`).
   Emitted skills carry a real `name` / `description` frontmatter block so
   they show up correctly in each tool's skill list, and every managed file
   records its source (`<url>@<short-sha>`) in its header.
3. **Moving the pin: `update`.** `agent-sync update` fetches the canonical
   remote, shows what changed since the current pin, and — on confirmation
   (or `--accept-update=<sha>` when non-interactive) — re-pins
   `commit` + `trusted_sha` and re-syncs in one locked step. It is
   fast-forward-only: a rewritten upstream history is refused unless you
   pass `--accept-rewritten-history=<sha>`. Use `--user` to update the
   `~` manifest.
4. **Safe by default.** Pinning, offline-strict, TOFU on `(URL, SHA)`
   pairs, reserved-prefix ownership with a ledger, and atomic swap with
   rollback — designed to fail closed.
5. **Capability-honest translation.** A tool-agnostic IR (`agents-md`,
   `rule`, `skill`, `command`, `plugin-reference`, `mcp-server-entry`) is
   translated by each adapter; per-target capability reports make
   lossy translations visible.

## Supported tools

Adapters translate the tool-agnostic IR into each tool's native files. Five
adapters ship bundled today; the rest are planned, and the `Planned (tier)`
label records the support tier each will land at.

| Tool | Status |
|------|--------|
| Claude Code | ✅ Bundled |
| Cursor | ✅ Bundled |
| Codex CLI | ✅ Bundled |
| Pi (`@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent`) | ✅ Bundled (agents-md; skill & command planned) |
| Antigravity (2.0 IDE + CLI; replaces Gemini CLI, retired 2026-06-18) | ✅ Bundled (full parity) |
| Windsurf | Planned (experimental) |
| LM Studio | Planned (experimental) |

## Requirements

- Go 1.25+
- Git (shelled out for network operations)

## Build

```bash
go build ./cmd/agent-sync
```

## Test

```bash
go test -race ./...
golangci-lint run
```

## Project layout

See [`docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md`](docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md)
for the complete repo layout. The short version:

```
cmd/agent-sync/    CLI entry point
internal/          All implementation packages; no external API yet
docs/              Requirements, plans, specs
```

## Contributing

See [`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md) and [`CLAUDE.md`](CLAUDE.md) for guidance when
working on `agent-sync` itself (including from inside a coding agent). These
files are **in-repo agent guidance for contributors**, not output of the
sync pipeline.

## License

Licensed under the [Apache License 2.0](LICENSE). See [`NOTICE`](NOTICE) for
attribution requirements.

   `.claude/rules/agent-sync/`, `.cursor/rules/agent-sync/`, `.codex/skills/agent-sync-<id>/`,
   `.agent/rules/agent-sync/` for Antigravity).
   Emitted skills carry a real `name` / `description` frontmatter block so
   they show up correctly in each tool's skill list, and every managed file
   records its source (`<url>@<short-sha>`) in its header.
3. **Moving the pin: `update`.** `agent-sync update` fetches the canonical
   remote, shows what changed since the current pin, and — on confirmation
   (or `--accept-update=<sha>` when non-interactive) — re-pins
   `commit` + `trusted_sha` and re-syncs in one locked step. It is
   fast-forward-only: a rewritten upstream history is refused unless you
   pass `--accept-rewritten-history=<sha>`. Use `--user` to update the
   `~` manifest.
4. **Safe by default.** Pinning, offline-strict, TOFU on `(URL, SHA)`
   pairs, reserved-prefix ownership with a ledger, and atomic swap with
   rollback — designed to fail closed.
5. **Capability-honest translation.** A tool-agnostic IR (`agents-md`,
   `rule`, `skill`, `command`, `plugin-reference`, `mcp-server-entry`) is
   translated by each adapter; per-target capability reports make
   lossy translations visible.

## Supported tools

Adapters translate the tool-agnostic IR into each tool's native files. Five
adapters ship bundled today; the rest are planned, and the `Planned (tier)`
label records the support tier each will land at.

| Tool | Status |
|------|--------|
| Claude Code | ✅ Bundled |
| Cursor | ✅ Bundled |
| Codex CLI | ✅ Bundled |
| Pi (`@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent`) | ✅ Bundled (agents-md; skill & command planned) |
| Antigravity (2.0 IDE + CLI; replaces Gemini CLI, retired 2026-06-18) | ✅ Bundled (full parity) |
| Windsurf | Planned (experimental) |
| LM Studio | Planned (experimental) |

## Requirements

- Go 1.25+
- Git (shelled out for network operations)

## Build

```bash
go build ./cmd/agent-sync
```

## Test

```bash
go test -race ./...
golangci-lint run
```

## Project layout

See [`docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md`](docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md)
for the complete repo layout. The short version:

```
cmd/agent-sync/    CLI entry point
internal/          All implementation packages; no external API yet
docs/              Requirements, plans, specs
```

## Contributing

See [`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md) and [`CLAUDE.md`](CLAUDE.md) for guidance when
working on `agent-sync` itself (including from inside a coding agent). These
files are **in-repo agent guidance for contributors**, not output of the
sync pipeline.

## License

Licensed under the [Apache License 2.0](LICENSE). See [`NOTICE`](NOTICE) for
attribution requirements.

Three Antigravity-related fixes to `README.md`:

1. **Intro** — the tool list said *"(Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI — and more to come)"*; now reads *"(Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI, Pi, Antigravity — with more planned)"*, matching the five bundled adapters.

The adapter-count table and the Antigravity row itself were already correct from the merged PR. The `Status: pre-alpha` line I left as-is — that's a maintainer positioning call, not an Antigravity fact; say the word if you'd like it revised now that v0.6.0 ships five adapters.

That completes the Antigravity work end to end: **built → reviewed → merged → released (v0.6.0) → README current.**

input.description: Read cursor and codex capability declarations

content.content:
======================== cursor capabilities.yaml ========================
name: cursor
version: "0.1"
contract_version: agent-sync/v1
reserved_prefix: .cursor

# concept_kinds is the authoritative per-IR-kind support declaration
# for the cursor adapter. The in-code map in capabilities.go must
# match this file kind-for-kind; capabilities_test.go enforces the
# parity. Add a kind here, add it in code; remove a kind here, remove
# it in code.
concept_kinds:
  agents-md:
    status: supported
    note: |
      Section-merged into the workspace-root AGENTS.md as a managed
      section between agent-sync:begin / agent-sync:end markers. Cursor reads
      AGENTS.md at the project root. Unlike claude, this writes the
      namesake file directly rather than a CLAUDE.md companion.
  rule:
    status: supported
    note: |
      Emitted at .cursor/rules/agent-sync/<id>.mdc with the managed-file
      header. v1 rules are frontmatter-less .mdc files (the IR strips
      frontmatter at decode); they behave as manual / agent-requested
      rules until IR frontmatter exposure lands. No paths: ward — that
      is a Claude Code bug with no Cursor equivalent.
  mcp-server-entry:
    status: supported
    note: |
      Merged into .cursor/mcp.json under /mcpServers/agentsync_<id> via
      the write_tool_owned op. A sidecar .cursor/.agent-sync-managed
      marker is written next to the strict-JSON file because JSON has
      no comment syntax.
  skill:
    status: unsupported
    note: |
      Cursor has no folder-per-skill / SKILL.md convention; skills are
      a Claude Code concept. Targeting skill at cursor surfaces a
      degradation warning and emits no files. If a future Cursor
      release adds a skills directory, flip to supported.
  command:
    status: unsupported
    note: |
      Cursor has no project-level custom slash-command concept
      analogous to Claude Code's .claude/commands/. Targeting command
      at cursor surfaces a degradation warning and emits no files.
  plugin-reference:
    status: unsupported
    note: |
      Cursor does not load project-level plugin-reference registries.
      Targeting plugin-reference at cursor surfaces a degradation
      warning and emits no files.
======================== codex capabilities.yaml ========================
name: codex
version: "0.1"
contract_version: agent-sync/v1
reserved_prefix: .codex

# concept_kinds is the authoritative per-IR-kind support declaration for the
# codex adapter. The in-code map in capabilities.go must match this file
# kind-for-kind; capabilities_test.go enforces the parity.
concept_kinds:
  agents-md:
    status: supported
    note: |
      Section-merged into the workspace-root AGENTS.md as a managed section
      between agent-sync:begin / agent-sync:end markers. Codex CLI reads AGENTS.md by
      walking from the repository root to the cwd. Note: Codex caps combined
      AGENTS.md size (project_doc_max_bytes, default 32 KiB) and prefers an
      AGENTS.override.md when present — see docs/adapters/codex.md.
  skill:
    status: supported
    note: |
      Emitted at .agents/skills/agent-sync-<id>/SKILL.md (plus assets). Codex scans
      the shared .agents/skills/ tree from cwd up to the repository root — this
      is a cross-tool convention, not a Codex-specific path, so the same skill
      tree is consumed by other agents (e.g. pi) without duplication.
  mcp-server-entry:
    status: supported
    note: |
      Merged into the project-local .codex/config.toml as a [mcp_servers.agentsync_<id>]
      table via the write_tool_owned op (toml-path locator). User content elsewhere
      in the file is preserved. If a Codex version does not load project-local MCP
      servers, the same table can be lifted to ~/.codex/config.toml.
  rule:
    status: unsupported
    note: |
      Codex CLI has no per-tool rule concept distinct from AGENTS.md. Targeting
      rule at codex surfaces a degradation warning and emits no files; consolidate
      rule content into agents-md IR nodes instead.
  command:
    status: unsupported
    note: |
      Codex custom prompts are deprecated in favor of skills, and live only in the
      user home (~/.codex/prompts/) — they are not shareable through a repository.
      Targeting command at codex surfaces a degradation warning; use a skill instead.
  plugin-reference:
    status: unsupported
    note: |
      Codex does not load project-level plugin-reference registries. Targeting
      plugin-reference at codex surfaces a degradation warning and emits no files.

content.text:
Before I answer, an important framing: in this codebase "full parity" doesn't mean "flip every flag to supported." The capability-honesty gate (the runtime fails a session where a *supported* kind emits zero ops) means an adapter may only claim a kind the tool **actually has a home for** — otherwise it must decline with a warning. So parity is bounded by what each tool genuinely supports, and the way to "make sure" is a **capability re-audit against current tool docs** (exactly the discipline that mattered for Antigravity — these notes go stale as tools ship features).

Here's the current gap picture:

| Kind | Cursor | Codex | Nature of the gap |
|---|---|---|---|
| agents-md | ✅ | ✅ | — |
| rule | ✅ | ❌ | Codex: "no rule concept distinct from AGENTS.md" — *by design* |
| skill | ❌ | ✅ | Cursor: "no SKILL.md convention" — **re-verify (stale risk)** |
| command | ❌ | ❌ | Cursor: "no slash-command concept" — **re-verify (stale risk)**; Codex: prompts deprecated + home-only — *by design* |
| mcp-server-entry | ✅ | ✅ | — |
| plugin-reference | ❌ | ❌ | *Universally declined* (claude, pi, antigravity too) — not part of parity |

The stale-risk cells are the ones worth verifying now — Cursor has been shipping fast. Let me check current docs rather than trust the notes (which predate recent releases).

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Cursor's custom slash commands and the .cursor/commands directory:

## Overview of Custom Slash Commands

Cursor allows you to create reusable prompts and quickly share them with your team, with commands stored in .cursor/commands/[command].md.

## How to Create Custom Commands

The concept stores custom prompts as Markdown files in .cursor/commands/, then access them instantly by typing / in Cursor's Agent input. Create Markdown files in the .cursor/commands/ directory, where the filename becomes the slash command name, and the file content is used as the prompt template.

Save standard Markdown files inside a .cursor/commands/ directory in your project root, so a file named lint-fix.md lets you type /lint-fix directly into your session to trigger that exact, structured instruction set.

## Scope (Project vs. Global)

For user-global commands (shared across all projects), place them in ~/.cursor/commands/. Project-level commands go in your project's .cursor/commands/ directory.

## Team Benefits

Commands live in your repository alongside your code—they're versioned, reviewed, and shared automatically, and when someone improves a command, the entire team benefits immediately.

This feature has been available since at least September 2025 and remains a key part of Cursor's workflow in 2026.

Here's When ...","url":"https://www.neura.market/directories/cursor/blog/devto-3273684"},{"title":"How to use Agent Skills in Cursor IDE? - Help - Cursor - Community Forum","url":"https://forum.cursor.com/t/how-to-use-agent-skills-in-cursor-ide/149860"},{"title":"Subagents, Skills, and Image Generation","url":"https://cursor.com/changelog/2-4"}]

Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about Cursor Agent Skills, SKILL.md format, and .cursor/skills support:

## Overview

Skills work across any agent that supports the Agent Skills standard. Skills are markdown files that teach Agent specialized workflows.

## File Structure and Location

Skills are stored as files and can be tracked in your repository, or installed via GitHub repository links. A .cursor/skills/ (or .agents/skills/) folder anywhere inside your repository is picked up, so monorepos can colocate skills with the package they apply to.

To create a skill manually, add a SKILL.md file in .cursor/skills/your-skill-name/

## SKILL.md Format

Reference scripts in your SKILL.md using relative paths from the skill root. Each skill folder must contain a SKILL.md file with frontmatter including a name and description.

## Scripts and Execution

Skills can include a scripts/ directory containing executable code that agents can run. Scripts can be written in any language—Bash, Python, JavaScript, or any other executable format supported by the agent implementation.

## Discovery and Invocation

When Cursor starts, it automatically discovers skills from skill directories and makes them available to Agent. The agent is presented with available skills and decides when they are relevant based on context. Skills can also be manually invoked by typing / in Agent chat and searching for the skill name.

## 2026 Support

Cursor now supports Agent Skills in the editor and CLI. Agents can discover and apply skills when domain-specific knowledge and workflows are relevant.

Based on the search results, here's information about OpenAI Codex CLI's AGENTS.md, rules, custom prompts, skills, and configuration:

## AGENTS.md - Custom Instructions

Codex concatenates AGENTS.md files from the root down with blank lines, with files closer to your current directory overriding earlier guidance because they appear later in the combined prompt.

Codex skips empty files and stops adding files once the combined size reaches the limit defined by project_doc_max_bytes (32 KiB by default). You can create persistent defaults in your Codex home directory so every repository inherits your working agreements.

## Rules and Working Agreements

AGENTS.md can include working agreements like: Always run `npm test` after modifying JavaScript files, prefer `pnpm` when installing dependencies, and ask for confirmation before adding new production dependencies.

## Skills and Configuration

The SKILL.md file must include name and description. Skills use progressive disclosure to manage context efficiently: Codex starts with each skill's name, description, and file path, and only loads the full SKILL.md instructions when it decides to use a skill.

Use [[skills.config]] entries in ~/.codex/config.toml to disable a skill without deleting it.

## Project 2026 Configuration

A useful way to think about Codex's approach is: start with the right task context, use AGENTS.md for durable guidance, configure Codex to match your workflow, connect external systems with MCP, turn repeated work into skills, and automate stable workflows.

Personal skills are stored in $HOME/.agents/skills, and shared team skills can be checked into .agents/skills inside a repository.

content.text:
This audit surfaced the key thing: **the "unsupported" notes are stale for Cursor, but accurate for Codex.** That distinction is the whole answer.

## Cursor — two *real, now-closeable* gaps

Both were written before Cursor shipped the features. As of 2026 Cursor has them:

- **Commands** → Cursor reads `.cursor/commands/<name>.md` (project-level, repo-versioned, `/name` triggers it) — shipped ~Sept 2025. ([Cursor docs](https://cursor.com/docs/cli/reference/slash-commands), [Reflag changelog](https://reflag.com/changelog/cursor-rules-commands))
- **Skills** → Cursor now supports Agent Skills: `SKILL.md` (name+description frontmatter) in **`.cursor/skills/` *or* `.agents/skills/`**, repo-tracked. ([Cursor Agent Skills docs](https://cursor.com/docs/context/skills), [changelog 2.4](https://cursor.com/changelog/2-4))

The skills one is a gift: Cursor reads the **same `.agents/skills/` shared tree** that codex/pi/antigravity already emit to. So Cursor skill support is mostly *wiring* — reuse the existing shared-subdir skill machinery and let Cursor co-own the tree (ADV-1 already makes multi-adapter co-ownership safe). Commands need a small new owned-subdir emitter (`.cursor/commands/agent-sync/`), structurally identical to Antigravity's workflow emitter.

**Closing these gets Cursor to genuine full parity** (agents-md, rule, skill, command, mcp).

## Codex — already at its honest ceiling

The Codex notes are *not* stale — verified against current OpenAI docs:

- **rule**: Codex has no rule concept distinct from AGENTS.md; working-agreements/rules live *in* AGENTS.md by design. ([AGENTS.md guide](https://developers.openai.com/codex/guides/agents-md))
- **command**: Codex deprecated custom prompts in favor of skills; "turn repeated work into skills." No repo-shareable command-file concept. ([Skills](https://developers.openai.com/codex/skills))

Forcing these to `supported` would emit files Codex never reads — which the **capability-honesty gate** (runtime fails a session where a supported kind emits zero real ops) is specifically designed to prevent. So Codex "parity" isn't a missing emitter; it's *routing*: rule content → an `agents-md` node, command content → a `skill`. That already works today. Codex is effectively complete at agents-md + skill + mcp.

(`plugin-reference` stays universally declined — claude, pi, and antigravity decline it too; it's not part of the parity bar.)

## How to "make sure" — the mechanism

1. **Capability re-audit** (what I just did): for each `unsupported` note, re-verify against *current* tool docs. Notes rot as tools ship — this is the same trap Antigravity avoided by researching fresh.
2. **Implement the genuinely-closeable gaps** — Cursor `command` + `skill` — with the Antigravity playbook: TDD emitters, flip capabilities.yaml + conceptKinds, coverage entries, docs, real `sync` e2e.
3. **Leave by-design gaps declined but accurately noted** — Codex rule/command; update the notes if the routing guidance can be sharper.

So the concrete work is **Cursor gets a full-parity PR; Codex gets (at most) a notes-accuracy touch-up.** Want me to spin up the Cursor parity work the same way I did Antigravity — plan → implement → PR?

aiTitle: Add Antigravity support with v2 API

content.text: Proceeding with the Cursor parity work. One design detail determines feasibility and I must verify it first (same rigor as before): **do Cursor commands support a namespaced subdirectory** like `.cursor/commands/agent-sync/`, or are they flat-only? If flat-only, they share a dir with user commands (the same file-leaf ownership problem that deferred pi's `command`). Let me check that plus Cursor's skill/command user-scope homes.

input.query: Cursor .cursor/commands subdirectory nested folder command name namespacing recursive

input.prompt: Where does Cursor read skills from? List exact directory paths: project-level (.cursor/skills/ and/or .agents/skills/?) and user-global. Does each skill live in its own folder with a SKILL.md? Does it read ~/.cursor/skills or ~/.agents/skills at user level? Quote exact paths.

content.content:
# Cursor Skill Directory Locations

According to the documentation, Cursor reads skills from these exact paths:

**Project-level:**
- `.agents/skills/`
- `.cursor/skills/`

**User-level (global):**
- `~/.agents/skills/`
- `~/.cursor/skills/`

The documentation also notes: "For compatibility, Cursor also loads skills from Claude and Codex directories: `.claude/skills/`, `.codex/skills/`, `~/.claude/skills/`, and `~/.codex/skills/`."

**Skill Structure:**

Yes, each skill resides in its own folder containing a `SKILL.md` file as the core requirement:

> "Each skill should be a folder containing a `SKILL.md` file"

Skills can optionally include `scripts/`, `references/`, and `assets/` subdirectories. Cursor also supports nested skill directories organized by category, with the skill identity determined by "the folder containing `SKILL.md`" rather than parent categories.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Cursor's `.cursor/commands` subdirectory and nested folder support:

## Current Support Status

Commands are currently supported only in three places: `.cursor/commands` at the project root, `~/.cursor/commands` in the home directory, and team commands in the dashboard. Nested `.cursor/commands` folders in subdirectories are not supported.

However, there is a difference in behavior between the IDE and CLI: Subfolders in the commands folder such as `.cursor/commands/development/` work in the Cursor IDE but don't appear as commands in the Cursor CLI.

## Contrast with Rules

It's worth noting that while nested commands have limited support, the behavior is different for rules. You can drop `.cursor/rules/` inside any subdirectory, and Cursor will apply those rules locally. Additionally, Cursor itself supports organizing rules in nested directories within `.cursor/rules/`, and should recursively scan and import all `.mdc` files within `.cursor/rules/`, including those in subdirectories.

In summary, nested command folders (particularly recursive subdirectory command namespacing) are not currently a fully supported feature in Cursor, though there are feature requests in the community for this functionality.

content.text:
Verifying first paid off — I need to correct something I told you. **Cursor commands are trickier than I implied.**

Cursor reads commands only from **flat** `.cursor/commands/` (project) and `~/.cursor/commands/` (home). Nested subfolders like `.cursor/commands/agent-sync/` **work in the Cursor IDE but not the Cursor CLI** ([forum: subfolders IDE-but-not-CLI](https://forum.cursor.com/t/subfolders-within-cursor-commands-folder-supported-in-ide-but-not-cli/154719), [nested commands not read](https://forum.cursor.com/t/commands-from-nested-folders-are-not-getting-read/146822)). That flat-dir constraint is the *same* ownership problem that deferred pi's `command`: to own individual files in a dir shared with the user's own commands, the engine needs **file-leaf swap** (it currently swaps whole owned subdirs only) — a cross-cutting change in the data-loss-critical swap code.

**Skill is unaffected and cleanly closeable** — Cursor reads `.agents/skills/` and `~/.agents/skills/` (the exact shared tree codex/pi/antigravity already emit to), each skill a folder with `SKILL.md` ([Cursor skills docs, confirmed via fetch](https://cursor.com/docs/context/skills)). So skill is mostly wiring into the existing shared-subdir machinery.

So Cursor's honest picture: **skill → clean `supported`; command → blocked on the flat-dir/file-leaf issue.** There's a real fork on how to handle command:

options.label: Partial (IDE) now (Recommended)
options.description: Emit to owned subdir .cursor/commands/agent-sync/ and declare capability `partial`: works in the Cursor IDE (the majority surface), not the CLI. Clean ownership, no risky engine change. Ships alongside skill in one Cursor PR. Documented CLI limitation.

options.label: Skill only; defer command
options.description: Ship Cursor skill as full `supported` now; leave command `unsupported` with an accurate note (blocked on engine file-leaf swap, shared with pi). Command lands later when the engine gains file-leaf swap (fixes pi + cursor together).

options.label: Do the engine file-leaf swap
options.description: Add file-leaf swap to internal/sync so command emits flat (.cursor/commands/<id>.md) and works in IDE + CLI, and fix pi command for free. Larger, touches data-loss-critical swap code; own effort with its own review.

content.content: Your questions have been answered: "How should Cursor `command` support be handled, given the flat-dir constraint?"="Do the engine file-leaf swap". You can now continue with these answers in mind.

aiTitle: Add Antigravity support with v2 API

content.text:
Bold call — the root-cause fix. That's the compound-engineering-correct choice: add **file-leaf swap** to the engine so agent-sync can own individual files inside a shared directory, which unblocks **Cursor command (IDE + CLI, flat)** *and* **pi command** together, plus **Cursor skill**.

But this touches the **data-loss-critical swap/ownership model** (AGENTS.md invariants #6 two-rename atomic swap, #7 ledger authority) — so it needs a deep plan and `ce-code-review`, not a quick adapter edit. Let me first map the current staging/swap/ledger/OutputMode machinery precisely, then plan against it.

input.prompt:
I'm working in the agent-sync Go project at /Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync. I need a precise, code-level map of the staging → atomic swap → ledger/ownership → orphan-reclaim machinery so I can add a new capability: **file-leaf swap** (owning individual files inside a directory that agent-sync does NOT own wholesale, i.e. a shared flat directory like `.cursor/commands/` or `.pi/prompts/`).

Today the engine supports these OutputModes (from pkg/adapterkit): `owned-subdir` (swapped wholesale), `shared-subdir` (manage only our agent-sync-<id> leaf DIRECTORIES inside a shared parent, e.g. `.agents/skills/`), and `tool-owned-entry` (merge into a user file via JSON pointer / TOML path / markdown section). The gap: there is no way to own individual FILES in a shared flat directory — staging currently `MkdirAll`s a directory leaf only. This blocks pi `command` (`.pi/prompts/<id>.md`) and cursor `command` (`.cursor/commands/<id>.md`).

Please investigate and report with exact file paths, line numbers, function signatures, and code excerpts:

1. **OutputMode definition & handling.** Where is `OutputMode` defined (pkg/adapterkit/types.go)? Enumerate every value and its string. Then trace where each mode is *handled* in the engine — grep for OutputModeOwnedSubdir / OutputModeSharedSubdir / OutputModeToolOwnedEntry across internal/. Show every switch/branch that keys on OutputMode.

2. **Staging.** Find `sync.Stage` (internal/sync/staging.go?) and everything it does. Where exactly does it `MkdirAll` a directory leaf? How does it build the staged tree for owned-subdir vs shared-subdir? Show the code.

3. **Atomic swap (invariant #6, two-rename).** internal/sync/swap.go — how does the two-rename atomic swap work for owned-subdir (swap whole dir) vs shared-subdir (swap individual leaf dirs)? What is the unit of swap? Show the swap function(s) and how the target path is chosen.

4. **Ledger authority (invariant #7).** internal/ledger — what does a ledger Entry record (Path, SHA256, mode?)? How is ownership tracked per emitted path? How does the ledger drive orphan detection?

5. **Orphan detection / reclaim.** How does the engine decide a previously-emitted path is now orphaned and delete it? For shared-subdir specifically, how does it avoid touching foreign/sibling leaves (the ADV-1 union-aware drift/orphan checks)? Show `ScanDriftUnion` / `warnOrphanLedgers` / plan.go orphan logic.

6. **Drift detection.** How does `validate` / `plan` detect drift for each mode (including `merge.DryMerge` for tool-owned)? Where would a file-leaf mode need a drift check?

7. **Path-safety gate.** internal/adapter/runtime.go `pathInDeclaredOutputs` — how does it validate an emitted op path against DeclaredOutputs, and how does it treat each OutputMode (esp. shared-subdir leaf containment)?

8. **The DeclaredOutput struct.** pkg/adapterkit/types.go — fields (Path, Mode, JSONPointer, SectionID, etc.). What would a file-leaf DeclaredOutput need?

9. **Where adapters emit skills into shared-subdir** — confirm codex/pi emitSkill produce OpMkdir + OpWriteFile ops and how the engine maps those to the shared-subdir staging/swap. This is the closest analog to what file-leaf needs.

10. Any existing tests for shared-subdir swap/ownership (internal/engine/shared_subdir_test.go, internal/sync/*_test.go) that I'd mirror for file-leaf.

Give me a clear picture of: what a new `file-leaf` (or `shared-file`) OutputMode would need to touch, and where the existing shared-subdir logic is the template vs where file-granularity differs (e.g. swap unit = file not dir, orphan reclaim per-file, no parent dir ownership). Read-only; do not modify anything.

content.text:
I now have a complete picture. Here is the code-level map.

---

# agent-sync: staging → swap → ledger → orphan-reclaim machinery, and where `file-leaf` slots in

## Executive summary

There are **two parallel `OutputMode` type systems** you must extend in lockstep: `pkg/adapterkit` (the public SDK adapters compile against) and `internal/adapter/contract` (the engine-internal mirror). Today the engine's write pipeline (`internal/engine/target.go:applyTarget`) has **three unit-of-work shapes**:

1. **Directory-tree swap** (owned-subdir + shared-subdir leaves) — stage a whole leaf dir, two-rename swap it into place.
2. **Single managed file** (`fileOutputs`/`fileType` path) — `StagedWrite` (temp+fsync+rename) a file whose path *equals* an owned-subdir prefix exactly.
3. **Tool-owned merge** (`merge.ApplyToFile`) — surgical in-place edit of a shared user file.

Crucially, **shape #2 already exists and is 80% of what `file-leaf` needs** — atomic per-file write, per-file ledger entry, per-file orphan delete. What's missing for `file-leaf` is the *containment model*: shape #2 requires the file path to BE a declared owned prefix, so agent-sync would have to "own" the parent. `file-leaf` needs the shared-subdir containment model (own only your files under a parent you don't own) but with the **file — not the `<parent>/<firstSegment>` leaf directory — as the swap/orphan/drift unit.**

---

## 1. OutputMode definition & handling

**Two definitions (keep in sync):**
- `pkg/adapterkit/types.go:41-52` — the SDK type. Values: `OutputModeOwnedSubdir="owned-subdir"` (44), `OutputModeToolOwnedEntry="tool-owned-entry"` (45), `OutputModeSharedSubdir="shared-subdir"` (51).
- `internal/adapter/contract/protocol.go:45-51` — the engine-internal mirror, identical string values. The engine works exclusively with `contract.OutputMode` (adapter InitializeResult is decoded into `contract.DeclaredOutput`).

`pkg/adapterkit/schema_parity_test.go` and `internal/adapter/contract/protocol_test.go` assert the two stay in sync — a new value must be added to both plus these tests.

**Every branch that keys on OutputMode in `internal/`:**
- `internal/engine/target.go:127` — `ownedSubdirs()` filters `Mode == OutputModeOwnedSubdir`.
- `internal/engine/target.go:152` — `sharedSubdirs()` filters `Mode == OutputModeSharedSubdir`.
- These two feed `emitOutcome.ownedPrefixes` / `sharedPrefixes` (target.go:99-101), which drive **everything downstream**. There is **no third `Mode ==` branch** for tool-owned in the engine — tool-owned ops are routed by **op kind** (`contract.OpWriteToolOwned`) in the `applyTarget` op switch (target.go:444), not by declared mode. So mode-keyed dispatch happens in exactly these two functions.
- Adapters set the mode in their `declaredOutputs()`: e.g. `internal/adapter/bundled/pi/capabilities.go:89-90`, `codex/capabilities.go:85`, `claude/capabilities.go:90`, `antigravity/capabilities.go:91`.

---

## 2. Staging — `sync.Stage`

`internal/sync/staging.go:34-43`:
```go
func Stage(root *fsroot.Root, parentRel, leaf string, m Meta) (stagingLeafRel string, err error) {
	if m.Timestamp == "" || m.SHA == "" { ... }
	rel := path.Join(stagingGenRel(parentRel, m), leaf)   // <parentRel>/.agent-sync-staging/<gen>/<leaf>
	if err := root.Inner().MkdirAll(rel, 0o755); err != nil { ... }   // <-- the directory-leaf MkdirAll
	return rel, nil
}
```
- Staging tree: `<parentRel>/.agent-sync-staging/<timestamp>-<sha>/<leaf>/`. `stagingDirName=".agent-sync-staging"` (line 12), `Meta.gen()` = `Timestamp + "-" + SHA` (line 24).
- **The `MkdirAll` at line 39 always creates a directory leaf.** This is the exact spot the task flags: `file-leaf` can't use `Stage` as-is because it materializes a dir named after the leaf, then the swap renames that dir onto the target.
- Owned-subdir vs shared-subdir build the *same* staged tree — `Stage` is mode-agnostic. The difference is entirely in **what `leaf`/`parentRel` are** (computed by the caller in `applyTarget`). For owned-subdir: `parentRel=path.Dir(pre)`, `leaf=path.Base(pre)` where `pre` is the whole owned dir. For shared-subdir: `pre` is the *managed leaf dir* `<shared>/<firstSegment>` (from `leafUnder`), so `parentRel=<shared>`, `leaf=agent-sync-<id>`.
- The actual file writes into the staged dir happen in `applyTarget` (target.go:548-578) via `root.StagedWrite(dst, ...)` and `MkdirAll` for nested dirs.

---

## 3. Atomic swap (two-rename, invariant #6) — `sync.Swap`

`internal/sync/swap.go:41-100`. **The unit of swap is one leaf directory** (`s.StagingLeafRel` → `s.PrefixRel`):
- Step 0 (line 62): write sentinel `StatusIntend`.
- Step 1 (line 69-73): if live `PrefixRel` exists, `rename(PrefixRel → PrefixRel+".old")`. Sentinel → `StatusStep1Done`.
- Step 2 (line 80): `rename(StagingLeafRel → PrefixRel)`. Sentinel → `StatusStep2Done`.
- Step 3 (line 96-98): `RemoveAll(prefixOld)`; drop sentinel only if `.old` is gone.

**Per-leaf sentinel keying (swap.go:17-53)** is the shared-subdir enabler: `sentinelRelFor(stagingLeafRel)` = `<gen-dir>/.state-<leaf base>` (line 21-23). One gen dir holds multiple leaves (shared-subdir stages several), each with an independent `.state-<leaf>` sentinel so one leaf's swap can't clobber another's recovery record.

**How the target path is chosen:** entirely by the caller. `applyTarget` sets `Sentinel{PrefixRel: pre, StagingLeafRel: stagingLeafRel}` (target.go:580-587) where `pre` is the effective owned prefix (whole dir for owned-subdir, `<shared>/<firstSegment>` leaf for shared-subdir).

**For owned-subdir**: `pre` = whole declared dir → swaps the entire subtree. **For shared-subdir**: `pre` = one `agent-sync-<id>` leaf dir → swaps only that leaf, never the shared parent (foreign siblings untouched because the parent is never renamed).

Recovery: `internal/sync/recover.go:56-73` (`reconcileGen`) walks *all* `.state-*` sentinels in a gen dir — one per leaf.

---

## 4. Ledger authority (invariant #7) — `internal/ledger`

`internal/ledger/types.go`:
- `Entry` (line 28-33): `Path string`, `SHA256 string`, `Size int64`, `EmittedAt time.Time`. **No mode field** — the ledger is a flat list of emitted paths + content hashes. Mode is re-derived at sync time from the adapter's live `DeclaredOutputs`.
- `Ledger` (line 36-40): `SchemaVersion`, `Target`, `[]Entry`. Lives at `.agent-sync/state/<target>.json`.
- Ownership per path = "is this path present in the target's ledger `Entries`". SHA256 is the emission-time hash; comparing on-disk bytes to it detects drift (never re-hashed on read).
- Written in `applyTarget` at target.go:612-618 (built from `newEntries`, one entry per emitted write_file / tool-owned op).

The ledger drives orphan detection by **ledger-diff only** (never a filesystem scan) — see §5.

---

## 5. Orphan detection / reclaim

**Pure ledger diff:** `internal/sync/orphans.go:18-31` `Orphans(old, next)` = paths in `old.Entries` not in `next.Entries`. `DeleteOrphans` (line 38-49) `Remove`s each (missing = silent no-op). But the actual orphan set used by sync is computed inline in `applyTarget`.

**In `applyTarget` (target.go:475-501):** iterate `oldLedger.Entries`; a path is deletable if:
- `ownerOf(effective, e.Path) != ""` (under a currently-effective owned prefix; line 477), AND
- not in `newEntries` (line 480), AND
- **not co-owned:** `!(underSharedLeaf(e.Path) && siblingKnown[e.Path])` (line 489) — the ADV-1 guard.

`--expect-deletions` guard runs **before any mutation** (target.go:499, `syncpkg.CheckExpectedDeletions`). Actual deletion runs **after** the new ledger is durable (target.go:624).

**Avoiding foreign/sibling leaves for shared-subdir — three mechanisms:**
1. `effectiveOwnedPrefixes` (target.go:193-214): the shared *parent* is never added; only `agent-sync-`managed leaves derived from this run's ops + prior ledger (`leafUnder`, target.go:166-184). Foreign siblings are never emitted and never in the ledger → invisible.
2. **Cross-adapter co-ownership (ADV-1):** `loadSiblingLedgerEntries` (target.go:668-681) unions all *other* configured targets' ledgers. `siblingKnown` (path-granular, line 263) blocks orphan-deleting a path a sibling still owns (line 489). `siblingLeaves` (leaf-granular, line 264-269) drives the **release filter** (target.go:300-327): a co-owned leaf this target no longer emits but a sibling still owns is dropped from `effective` (not swap-emptied, not deleted). There's a fail-closed guard (target.go:315-321) for divergent co-emission.
3. `warnOrphanLedgers` (`internal/engine/engine.go:65-106`): read-only; warns (never deletes) when a `.agent-sync/state/<target>.json` exists for a target no longer in the manifest. Reclaim is deferred to `agent-sync unmanage`.

`ScanDriftUnion` (`internal/sync/drift.go:28-46`): refuses if any file under `prefixRel` is in *neither* this target's ledger nor `extra` (sibling entries). This is the drift half of ADV-1.

---

## 6. Drift detection per mode — `internal/engine/plan.go`

`planTarget` (plan.go:21-156) mirrors `applyTarget` without mutating:
- Builds the same `effective` set (plan.go:45) and `siblingKnown` (plan.go:54-65).
- **owned-subdir / shared-subdir (write_file):** for each desired path, compare against ledger SHA (`WouldCreate`/`WouldUpdate`, plan.go:107-113); if ledger says unchanged, `readHash` on-disk to detect `OutOfBand` edits (plan.go:114-132).
- **tool-owned:** `merge.DryMerge(req.Root, o.Path, entry)` (plan.go:85) runs the same idempotent merge as sync and diffs against on-disk bytes → `WouldCreate`/`WouldUpdate` (plan.go:96-103). `mergeEntryFrom` (plan.go:164-166) builds the entry.
- **deletions:** ledger paths under `effective` no longer desired, minus co-owned siblings (plan.go:135-148) — mirrors the sync orphan guard exactly to avoid phantom deletions in `validate`.

The at-sync drift *gate* is in `applyTarget:335-352`: per effective prefix, `ScanDriftUnion` (shared leaf) or `ScanDrift` (owned), with `--adopt-prefix` escape.

**Where `file-leaf` needs a drift check:** a `file-leaf` output is a single file, not a directory to walk. `ScanDrift`/`ScanDriftUnion` walk a dir (`walkFiles`) — for `file-leaf` you'd want a **per-file** drift check: compare the on-disk file's hash against the ledger entry (like the write_file `OutOfBand` logic in plan.go:114-131), and — critically — **not walk the shared parent** (that would flag every foreign file in `.cursor/commands/`). This is the key divergence from shared-subdir, which *can* walk its `agent-sync-<id>` leaf dir because that dir is wholly agent-sync-owned.

---

## 7. Path-safety gate — `pathInDeclaredOutputs`

`internal/adapter/runtime.go:509-554`. Called per op in `Emit` (runtime.go:190). Rejects: empty path, absolute path, backslash, Windows volume prefix (`reWindowsVolumePrefix`, line 23), `../` escapes. Then (line 534-551): for each declared output, `path.Clean` both sides; accept if `clean == declClean || strings.HasPrefix(clean, declClean+"/")` (a `.` declared output = workspace root, accepts everything).

**Key point:** the gate is **mode-agnostic prefix containment** — it does *not* distinguish shared-subdir leaf containment from owned-subdir. A shared-subdir declares `.agents/skills` and any op path under it passes. So a `file-leaf` output declaring `.cursor/commands` would **already pass** the gate for `.cursor/commands/foo.md` with no change needed — *unless* you want tighter enforcement (e.g. reject a `file-leaf` op that names a subdirectory rather than a direct-child file). If you want that, this is where a mode-aware branch would go, reading `decl.Mode`.

---

## 8. The `DeclaredOutput` struct

`pkg/adapterkit/types.go:159-165` (mirror: `internal/adapter/contract` decodes the same JSON):
```go
type DeclaredOutput struct {
	Path        string          `json:"path"`
	Mode        OutputMode      `json:"mode"`
	JSONPointer *string         `json:"json_pointer,omitempty"`
	SectionID   *string         `json:"section_id,omitempty"`
	Meta        json.RawMessage `json:"_meta,omitempty"`
}
```
**What `file-leaf` needs:** most likely `{Path: ".cursor/commands", Mode: OutputModeFileLeaf}` — i.e. Path = the shared *parent dir*, with the mode signalling "I own individual direct-child files, not the dir and not subtrees." No new struct field is strictly required if the file identity comes from the op paths (like shared-subdir derives leaves from ops). Optionally a glob/extension filter field (e.g. `LeafPattern *string`) if you want to constrain which children are agent-sync's — but the ledger-driven ownership model means you may not need it (ownership = "in the ledger").

---

## 9. Where adapters emit skills into shared-subdir (closest analog)

`internal/adapter/bundled/pi/emit_reserved.go:35-110` `emitSkill` (byte-identical to codex's, per its doc comment). Per skill node it emits:
- `OpMkdir{Path: ".agents/skills/agent-sync-<id>", Mode: 0o755}` (line 42)
- `OpWriteFile{Path: ".agents/skills/agent-sync-<id>/SKILL.md", ...}` (line 44-52)
- `OpWriteFile` per asset (line 100-107)

**How the engine maps these to shared-subdir staging/swap:** In `applyTarget`, the declared `.agents/skills` shared prefix + these op paths flow through `effectiveOwnedPrefixes`→`leafUnder` (target.go:193-214, 166-184), which extracts the leaf `.agents/skills/agent-sync-<id>` (first segment below the shared parent). That leaf becomes `pre` in the stage+swap loop (target.go:520-591): `parentRel=.agents/skills`, `leaf=agent-sync-<id>`, `Stage` mkdirs the staging leaf, writes are re-rooted via `strings.TrimPrefix(wf.Path, pre+"/")` (target.go:562-563), then `Swap` renames the leaf into place. The `OpMkdir` for the skill dir itself is a no-op relative-to-pre (target.go:549-551, `rel==""` → continue).

**Where `file-leaf` differs from this analog:**
- skill emits `OpMkdir` + `OpWriteFile`s into a **leaf directory**; `file-leaf` emits **just `OpWriteFile`** for `<parent>/<id>.md` (no mkdir, no subtree).
- shared-subdir's swap unit = the `agent-sync-<id>` *directory*; `file-leaf`'s swap unit = the *file* `<id>.md`. `leafUnder` returns `<shared>/<firstSegment>` which for a flat file would be the file itself (`<parent>/foo.md`) — so `leafUnder` almost works, but downstream `Stage`+`Swap` treat that as a *directory* (MkdirAll + dir rename), which is wrong for a file. You need a file-granular stage/swap (reuse the existing `fileOutputs`/`StagedWrite` path, §11).

---

## 10. Existing tests to mirror

- `internal/engine/shared_subdir_test.go` — the canonical suite. Mirror these directly for `file-leaf`:
  - `TestSync_SharedSubdir_PreservesForeignSkill` (line 49) → foreign *file* in `.cursor/commands/` survives add+remove.
  - `TestSync_SharedSubdir_CodexPreservesForeignSkill` (103), `..._UpdatePreservesForeignSkill` (148).
  - `TestSync_SharedSubdir_CodexAndPiCoOwn` (192) → if two adapters can co-own a flat command file (pi + cursor both `.pi/prompts` vs `.cursor/commands` are different dirs, so co-ownership may not apply — but mirror the structure if any two share a flat dir).
  - `TestSync_SharedSubdir_OneTargetRemovePreservesSiblingSkill` (268), `TestPlan_SharedSubdir_CoOwnedRelease_NoFalseDelete` (319), `..._CoOwnedLeaf_ForeignFileStillDrift` (368) → **critical**: a foreign file dropped in the shared flat dir must trip drift, not be clobbered.
  - `..._CorruptSiblingLedgerFailsClosed` (462), `..._CoOwnedUpdate` (419).
  - Helpers `assertSkillContent` (507), `assertFileBytes` (521).
- `internal/sync/shared_leaf_test.go:14` `TestRecover_MultiLeafSentinelsDoNotCollide` — mirror for multiple `file-leaf` files staged in one gen dir (per-file sentinels must not collide). If `file-leaf` uses `StagedWrite` instead of stage-dir+Swap, this test may not apply — depends on your implementation choice (§11).
- `internal/sync/drift_test.go`, `internal/sync/orphans_test.go`, `internal/sync/swap_test.go` for unit-level coverage.
- `internal/engine/target_helpers_test.go` for `effectiveOwnedPrefixes`/`leafUnder`/`ownerOf` unit tests.

---

## What a new `file-leaf` OutputMode must touch

**Add the mode (2 places + parity tests):**
- `pkg/adapterkit/types.go:43-52` and `internal/adapter/contract/protocol.go:47-51` — add `OutputModeFileLeaf`/`OutputModeSharedFile`.
- `pkg/adapterkit/schema_parity_test.go`, `internal/adapter/contract/protocol_test.go` — extend parity assertions.

**Engine dispatch (`internal/engine/target.go`):**
- Add a `fileLeafPrefixes()` filter alongside `ownedSubdirs`/`sharedSubdirs` (target.go:124-160), and a field on `emitOutcome` (target.go:30-35).
- Decide the ownership/containment helper: shared-subdir uses `leafUnder` returning a *directory* leaf; `file-leaf` wants the **file path itself** as the unit. Add a `fileLeafUnder(prefixes, p)` that returns `p` when `p` is a direct child file of a declared file-leaf parent (reject subdirs).
- Extend `effectiveOwnedPrefixes` (target.go:193-214) — or add a parallel `effectiveFileLeaves` — so file-leaf files (from ops + prior ledger) are tracked for drift/orphan without ever adding the shared parent.
- **Reuse the existing single-file write path** (`fileOutputs`/`fileType`/`StagedWrite`, target.go:405-408, 503-516) as the swap mechanism — it already does atomic temp+fsync+rename per file and requires no directory swap. The change is *classification*: currently a write is treated as a single file only when `o.Path == pre` (target.go:421, the owned prefix IS the file). For file-leaf, classify as single-file when the path is a file-leaf child, even though the declared prefix (the parent dir) is not the file. This is the cleanest integration point — you sidestep `Stage`/`Swap`/sentinels entirely.
- Orphan set (target.go:475-493): file-leaf files already work via `ownerOf`/`newEntries` diff **if** `ownerOf`(or an added `fileLeafOwnerOf`) recognizes file-leaf parents. Per-file delete is already what `DeleteOrphans` does.
- Co-ownership (ADV-1): only needed if two targets share the *same* flat dir + filenames. `.pi/prompts/` (pi) and `.cursor/commands/` (cursor) are distinct dirs → likely no co-ownership, so you can skip the `siblingLeaves`/release-filter complexity for the first cut (but keep `siblingKnown` if any overlap exists).

**Drift (`internal/engine/plan.go` + `internal/sync/drift.go`):**
- Add a **per-file** drift check (compare on-disk hash to ledger entry) instead of `ScanDrift`/`ScanDriftUnion`'s directory walk — you must NOT walk the shared parent (`.cursor/commands/`) or every foreign command trips drift. Reuse the `OutOfBand` on-disk-hash comparison already in `planTarget` (plan.go:114-131) and add the equivalent gate in `applyTarget` (analogous to target.go:335-352 but per-file, no walk).

**Path-safety gate (`internal/adapter/runtime.go:509`):** works as-is (prefix containment), but optionally add a mode-aware branch to reject file-leaf op paths that name a subdirectory rather than a direct-child file.

**Adapters:** implement `emitCommand` in `internal/adapter/bundled/pi/` and `internal/adapter/bundled/cursor/` (today `command` is `Unsupported` — pi `capabilities.go:43` + `emit_unsupported.go:20`; cursor `capabilities.go:47` + `emit_unsupported.go:16`). Each emits a single `OpWriteFile{Path: "<parent>/<id>.md"}` (no `OpMkdir`), and adds a `{Path: ".pi/prompts"|".cursor/commands", Mode: OutputModeFileLeaf}` to `declaredOutputs()` (pi `capabilities.go:81-92`, cursor equivalent). Flip `ir.KindCommand` to `Supported` and remove it from the unsupported emit switch (pi `emit.go:185`, cursor `emit.go:268`).

**The single most important divergence from shared-subdir:** swap/drift/orphan unit = **file, not directory**. shared-subdir owns a whole `agent-sync-<id>` subdir it can walk and dir-swap; `file-leaf` owns bare files in a directory full of foreign siblings it must never walk or swap. The existing `fileOutputs`/`StagedWrite` path (shape #2) is the correct atomic primitive; the work is teaching the classification/containment layer (`ownerOf`, `effectiveOwnedPrefixes`, the drift gate) to recognize file-leaf children of an un-owned parent.
